stack_of_cups.title: Stack of Cups Challenge
stack_of_cups.standardDescription: Construct a function to model a linear relationship between two quantities and interpret the rate of change and initial value.
stack_of_cups.description: Teach AI to understand linear patterns in stacked cups
stack_of_cups.descriptionES: Enseña a la IA a comprender patrones lineales en vasos apilados
stack_of_cups.imageUrl: data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg width='400' height='200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%3E%3Crect fill='%23f3f4f6' width='400' height='200'/%3E%3Ctext x='50%25' y='30%25' text-anchor='middle' fill='%23374151' font-size='14'%3E2 cups = 16 cm%3C/text%3E%3Ctext x='50%25' y='50%25' text-anchor='middle' fill='%23374151' font-size='14'%3E4 cups = 20 cm%3C/text%3E%3Ctext x='50%25' y='70%25' text-anchor='middle' fill='%23374151' font-size='14'%3E8 cups = 28 cm%3C/text%3E%3C/svg%3E
stack_of_cups.problemStatement: A cafeteria manager is stacking identical plastic cups. She records: 2 cups = 16cm, 4 cups = 20cm, 8 cups = 28cm. Determine the pattern and create a formula for the height of n cups.
stack_of_cups.teachingPrompt: Help Zippy understand how cups nest together and how to model the linear relationship between number of cups and total height.
stack_of_cups.correctSolutionPathway: Cups nest inside each other. Base cup is 14cm tall. Each additional cup adds only its 2cm rim. Formula: h = 14 + 2(n-1) or h = 2n + 12
misconceptions: The Proportionality Error: Assuming direct variation (y = kx). 2 cups = 16cm so 4 cups = 32cm, ignoring nesting.
Misidentifying the Rate: Using the height of the first cup (14cm) as the rate of change instead of the rim (2cm).
n vs. n-1 Error: Off-by-one error by adding rim height for every cup including the first.
Cannot generalize: Can calculate specific cases but struggles to create formula
stack_of_cups.aiIntro:
Hi! I'm Zippy! 🎉

I see 2 cups = 16cm and 4 cups = 20cm.

If 1 cup = 8cm, then 8 cups = 64cm... but the picture shows 28cm! 🤔

Can you help me figure out what's happening?
stack_of_cups.aiIntroES:
¡Hola! ¡Soy Zippy! 🎉

Veo que 2 vasos = 16cm y 4 vasos = 20cm.

Si 1 vaso = 8cm, entonces 8 vasos = 64cm... ¡pero la imagen muestra 28cm! 🤔

¿Puedes ayudarme a entender qué está pasando?

sections.studentPrompt: A cafeteria manager is stacking identical plastic cups. She records: 2 cups = 16cm, 4 cups = 20cm, 8 cups = 28cm. Look at the table above. When you increase the number of cups from 2 to 4, what is the change in height? When you increase from 4 to 8, what is the change in height? Describe what physical part of the cup is represented by this change.
misconceptions: The Proportionality Error: Assuming direct variation (y = kx), ignoring nesting structure
Misidentifying the Rate: Using full cup height (14cm) as rate instead of rim (2cm)
n vs. n-1 Error: Off-by-one error in formula leading to incorrect heights
Cannot generalize: Can compute specific cases but struggles to create a formula
sections.patternRecognition: Compare the heights as cups increase. What changes each time you add cups? What stays the same?
sections.generalization: Always/Sometimes/Never: The total height of a stack of cups is double the height of a stack with half as many cups. Justify your answer using the data from the table.
sections.inference: A different brand of 'Mega-Cups' is introduced. The first cup is 20cm tall. When you nest a second cup inside it, the total height becomes 23cm. Create a formula for the height (h) of (n) Mega-Cups. What would be the height of 10 Mega-Cups?

claims: Student can identify the constant rate of change in a linear context
The relationship between number of cups and height is linear
mappingData.evidence: Student identifies the 2cm increase per cup (the rim height). Providing non-consecutive values (2, 4, 8) forces students to calculate change over an interval.
mappingData.criticalThinking: Critical thinking is revealed if a student checks their generated formula against all three data points. Self-correction and data validation demonstrate deeper understanding.

smoothie_recipe.title: Smoothie Recipe Ratios
smoothie_recipe.standardDescription: Use ratio and rate reasoning to solve real-world and mathematical problems, e.g., by reasoning about tables of equivalent ratios.
smoothie_recipe.description: Teach AI about ratio and proportional reasoning with recipes
smoothie_recipe.descriptionES: Enseña a la IA sobre razones y razonamiento proporcional con recetas
smoothie_recipe.imageUrl: data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg width='400' height='280' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%3E%3Crect fill='%23fef3c7' width='400' height='280'/%3E%3Ctext x='200' y='30' text-anchor='middle' fill='%23b91c1c' font-size='18' font-weight='bold'%3E🍓 Smoothie Recipe 🥤%3C/text%3E%3Ctext x='200' y='60' text-anchor='middle' fill='%23059669' font-size='14' font-weight='bold'%3EOriginal Recipe%3C/text%3E%3Ctext x='100' y='90' text-anchor='middle' fill='%23dc2626' font-size='32'%3E🍓🍓%3C/text%3E%3Ctext x='100' y='115' text-anchor='middle' fill='%23374151' font-size='12'%3E2 cups%3C/text%3E%3Ctext x='200' y='102' text-anchor='middle' fill='%23374151' font-size='20'%3E:%3C/text%3E%3Ctext x='300' y='90' text-anchor='middle' fill='%2393c5fd' font-size='32'%3E🥛🥛🥛%3C/text%3E%3Ctext x='300' y='115' text-anchor='middle' fill='%23374151' font-size='12'%3E3 cups%3C/text%3E%3Crect x='50' y='130' width='300' height='2' fill='%23d1d5db'/%3E%3Ctext x='200' y='160' text-anchor='middle' fill='%23dc2626' font-size='13' font-weight='bold'%3ERatio: 2 to 3%3C/text%3E%3Ctext x='200' y='185' text-anchor='middle' fill='%23374151' font-size='11'%3ETo double: multiply BOTH by 2%3C/text%3E%3Ctext x='200' y='210' text-anchor='middle' fill='%23374151' font-size='11'%3E🍓🍓 × 2 = 🍓🍓🍓🍓 (4 cups)%3C/text%3E%3Ctext x='200' y='230' text-anchor='middle' fill='%23374151' font-size='11'%3E🥛🥛🥛 × 2 = 🥛🥛🥛🥛🥛🥛 (6 cups)%3C/text%3E%3Ctext x='200' y='260' text-anchor='middle' fill='%23374151' font-size='10' style='font-style:italic'%3EKeeps the same taste!%3C/text%3E%3C/svg%3E
smoothie_recipe.problemStatement: A 'Berry Blast' smoothie recipe uses 2 cups of strawberries and 3 cups of yogurt. Complete a table to make Single, Double, Half, and Five Batches of the same smoothie while keeping the same taste.
smoothie_recipe.teachingPrompt: Help Zippy understand how ratios work and how to scale recipes multiplicatively (not additively).
smoothie_recipe.correctSolutionPathway: Ratios scale multiplicatively. Double: 4 strawberries, 6 yogurt. Half: 1 strawberry, 1.5 yogurt. Five: 10 strawberries, 15 yogurt. Unit rate: 2/3 cup strawberries per cup yogurt.
misconceptions: Additive Reasoning: Thinking adding 1 cup of each ingredient keeps the taste the same (moving from 2:3 to 3:4).
Part-to-Whole Confusion: Mistaking the ratio of strawberries to yogurt (2/3) for the fraction of strawberries in the whole smoothie (2/5).
Scaling Error: Scaling only one ingredient and not the other, or using different multipliers.
Cannot generalize: Struggles to apply ratio concept to different scaling factors
smoothie_recipe.aiIntro:
Hi! I'm Zippy! 🎉

Recipe: 2 cups strawberries + 3 cups yogurt

To double it... 2+3=5, so double = 10 total? Maybe 5 strawberries and 5 yogurt? 🤔

Can you help me understand how to double recipes?
smoothie_recipe.aiIntroES:
¡Hola! ¡Soy Zippy! 🎉

Receta: 2 tazas de fresas + 3 tazas de yogur

Para duplicarlo... 2+3=5, ¿entonces el doble = 10 total? ¿Tal vez 5 fresas y 5 yogur? 🤔

¿Puedes ayudarme a entender cómo duplicar recetas?

sections.studentPrompt: A recipe for a 'Berry Blast' smoothie uses 2 cups of strawberries and 3 cups of yogurt. Complete the table to make Single Batch (2, 3), Double Batch (4, 6), Half Batch (?, ?), and Five Batches (?, ?). Look at the relationship between the two ingredients in every row. What mathematical pattern ensures the 'taste' stays exactly the same?
misconceptions: Additive Reasoning: Thinking adding 1 cup of each ingredient keeps the taste the same
Part-to-Whole Confusion: Mistaking ratio of ingredients for fraction of whole smoothie
Scaling Error: Scaling only one ingredient or using different multipliers
No generalization: Struggles to apply the same idea to other scale factors
sections.patternRecognition: If you multiply the number of smoothies by 2, what must happen to EACH ingredient to keep the flavor the same?
sections.generalization: Always, Sometimes, or Never: If you want to increase a recipe while keeping the same taste, you should add 1 cup of each ingredient for every new person you are serving. Justify your answer using the Berry Blast recipe as an example.
sections.inference: A chef has 10 cups of yogurt and wants to use all of it to make a giant Berry Blast smoothie. Using the 2:3 ratio, how many cups of strawberries are needed? If she only has 10 cups of strawberries, how much yogurt would she need to maintain the same taste?

claims: Student can maintain equivalent ratios through scaling
Scaling is multiplicative, not additive
mappingData.evidence: Correct completion of the Half and Five Batch rows in the table. Requiring a 'Half Batch' prevents reliance on addition (2+2+2) and forces multiplicative thinking.
mappingData.criticalThinking: Student uses equivalent ratios (and optionally unit rate of 1.5 cups yogurt per 1 cup strawberry) to verify scaled rows, demonstrating high-level conceptual mapping.

basketball_heights.title: The Basketball Team Heights
basketball_heights.titleES: Las Alturas del Equipo de Baloncesto
basketball_heights.standardDescription: Understand that a set of data collected to answer a statistical question has a distribution
basketball_heights.description: Analyze height data and use statistical measures to understand team composition
basketball_heights.descriptionES: Analizar datos de altura y usar medidas estadísticas para entender la composición del equipo
basketball_heights.problemStatement: The basketball coach collected height data for all team members: 65, 66, 66, 67, 67, 67, 68, 68, 71, 73 inches. Use statistical measures (mean, median, range) to analyze the distribution and describe what the data tells us about the team.
basketball_heights.teachingPrompt: Help Zippy understand how to summarize a data set using center (mean, median) and spread (range), and why different measures give different pictures of the team.
basketball_heights.correctSolutionPathway: Mean = 67.8 inches. Median = 67 inches. Range = 73 - 65 = 8 inches. Most players cluster between 65-68 inches, with two taller players (71, 73) pulling the mean up. The median (67) is more representative of a typical player height than the mean (67.8) because of the two outliers.
misconceptions: Thinking the mean always represents the 'typical' value
Confusing range (max-min) with interquartile range
Not recognizing that outliers pull the mean away from center
Thinking all averages give the same result
basketball_heights.aiIntro:
Hi! I'm Zippy! 🎉

The basketball coach gave me the team heights: 65, 66, 66, 67, 67, 67, 68, 68, 71, 73 inches.

To find the average I just add them all up... that's 678, and there are 10 players, so the average is 67.8 inches.

So every player is about 67.8 inches tall, right? 🤔

Can you help me understand what this data really tells us about the team?
basketball_heights.aiIntroES:
¡Hola! ¡Soy Zippy! 🎉

El entrenador de baloncesto me dio las alturas del equipo: 65, 66, 66, 67, 67, 67, 68, 68, 71, 73 pulgadas.

Para encontrar el promedio sumo todo... eso es 678, y hay 10 jugadores, así que el promedio es 67.8 pulgadas.

¿Entonces todos los jugadores miden aproximadamente 67.8 pulgadas? 🤔

¿Puedes ayudarme a entender qué nos dicen realmente estos datos sobre el equipo?

sections.studentPrompt: The basketball coach has collected height data for all team members. Your task is to help analyze this data to understand the team better.
misconceptions: Students may think the average (mean) is always the most representative value
Students might not recognize that outliers significantly affect the mean
Students may confuse range with interquartile range
sections.patternRecognition: Look at the height distribution. What patterns do you notice?
sections.generalization: Always/Sometimes/Never: The tallest player on a team is always more than 6 inches taller than the shortest player.
sections.inference: If a new player joins the team and is 72 inches tall, how would this affect the team's average height?

claims: The team has mostly players of similar height
There are a few very tall players
mappingData.evidence: Height data shows 8 players between 65-68 inches, with 2 players over 70 inches
mappingData.criticalThinking: Student demonstrates understanding of distribution and central tendency

solar_energy.title: Solar Panel Energy
solar_energy.standardDescription: Write and evaluate numerical expressions involving whole-number exponents
solar_energy.description: Use expressions to calculate energy production from solar panels
solar_energy.descriptionES: Usar expresiones para calcular la producción de energía de paneles solares
solar_energy.problemStatement: A single solar panel produces 5 kWh per day. If the number of panels doubles each year (starting with 1 panel), write an expression using exponents for the energy produced after n years. How much energy is produced daily after 4 years?
solar_energy.teachingPrompt: Help Zippy understand the difference between multiplication and exponents, and how exponential growth models real-world situations like solar panel expansion.
solar_energy.correctSolutionPathway: After n years there are 2^n panels. Each produces 5 kWh, so total daily energy = 5 × 2^n. After 4 years: 5 × 2^4 = 5 × 16 = 80 kWh per day.
misconceptions: Treating exponents as multiplication: 2^4 = 2 × 4 = 8 instead of 16
Not following order of operations: computing 5 × 2 first, then raising to power
Confusing linear growth (adding panels) with exponential growth (doubling)
Struggling to write expressions before evaluating them
solar_energy.aiIntro:
Hi! I'm Zippy! 🎉

So we start with 1 solar panel that makes 5 kWh per day, and the panels double each year...

After 4 years, the panels double 4 times, so that's 2 × 4 = 8 panels? And 8 × 5 = 40 kWh!

Wait, my friend said it should be way more than 40... 🤔

Can you help me figure out how "doubling" really works?
solar_energy.aiIntroES:
¡Hola! ¡Soy Zippy! 🎉

Empezamos con 1 panel solar que produce 5 kWh por día, y los paneles se duplican cada año...

Después de 4 años, los paneles se duplican 4 veces, ¿así que son 2 × 4 = 8 paneles? ¡Y 8 × 5 = 40 kWh!

Espera, mi amigo dijo que debería ser mucho más que 40... 🤔

¿Puedes ayudarme a entender cómo funciona realmente "duplicar"?

sections.studentPrompt: A solar panel produces energy based on hours of sunlight. Write an expression to calculate total energy production over different time periods.
misconceptions: Students may not understand order of operations with exponents
Students might treat exponents as multiplication
Students may struggle with writing expressions before evaluating them
sections.patternRecognition: How does doubling the number of solar panels affect total energy? What pattern do you see?
sections.generalization: Always/Sometimes/Never: Increasing the exponent in an expression always increases the value.
sections.inference: If we add 3 more solar panels, predict how the expression would change.

claims: Energy production grows exponentially with more panels
Time affects total output linearly
mappingData.evidence: Expression shows 2^n × hours, where n is number of panels
mappingData.criticalThinking: Student connects exponential growth to real-world context

unit_rates.title: Comparing Unit Rates
unit_rates.standardDescription: Use ratio and rate reasoning to solve problems
unit_rates.description: Find and compare unit rates to determine the better buy
unit_rates.descriptionES: Encontrar y comparar tasas unitarias para determinar la mejor compra
unit_rates.problemStatement: Store A sells 3 notebooks for $4.50. Store B sells 5 notebooks for $7.00. Which store has the better price per notebook? How do you know?
unit_rates.teachingPrompt: Help Zippy understand what a unit rate is, how to calculate it by dividing, and how to use it to compare deals fairly.
unit_rates.correctSolutionPathway: Store A: $4.50 ÷ 3 = $1.50 per notebook. Store B: $7.00 ÷ 5 = $1.40 per notebook. Store B is cheaper per notebook. The unit rate lets you compare fairly even when the quantities differ.
misconceptions: Comparing total prices instead of unit rates (Store A is cheaper because $4.50 < $7.00)
Dividing the wrong way (3 ÷ $4.50 instead of $4.50 ÷ 3)
Not understanding that lower unit price = better deal
Thinking you need the same quantity to compare
unit_rates.aiIntro:
Hi! I'm Zippy! 🎉

Store A has 3 notebooks for $4.50 and Store B has 5 notebooks for $7.00.

Well, $4.50 is less than $7.00, so Store A must be the better deal... right? 🤔

But Store B gives you more notebooks... I'm confused about how to compare when the amounts are different!

Can you teach me a fair way to compare these?
unit_rates.aiIntroES:
¡Hola! ¡Soy Zippy! 🎉

La Tienda A tiene 3 cuadernos por $4.50 y la Tienda B tiene 5 cuadernos por $7.00.

Bueno, $4.50 es menos que $7.00, así que la Tienda A debe ser la mejor oferta... ¿verdad? 🤔

Pero la Tienda B te da más cuadernos... ¡Estoy confundido sobre cómo comparar cuando las cantidades son diferentes!

¿Puedes enseñarme una forma justa de comparar estas?

sections.studentPrompt: Store A sells 3 notebooks for $4.50. Store B sells 5 notebooks for $7.00. Which store has the better price per notebook?
misconceptions: Students may compare total prices instead of unit rates
Students might not divide correctly to find unit price
Students may not understand that lower unit rate means better deal
sections.patternRecognition: What relationship do you see between the number of items and total cost?
sections.generalization: Always/Sometimes/Never: Buying more items always results in a lower unit price.
sections.inference: If Store A offered 4 notebooks for $6.00, predict whether this would be better or worse than Store B.

claims: Store A has a unit rate of $1.50 per notebook
Store B has a unit rate of $1.40 per notebook
mappingData.criticalThinking: Student uses unit rate reasoning to make informed consumer decisions

snack_pack_distribution.title: Snack Pack Distribution
snack_pack_distribution.description: A school is packing snack bags for a field trip. Each bag has 4 apple slices and 3 grape bunches. If the school makes 5 bags, explain how you could fi
snack_pack_distribution.problemStatement:
A school is packing snack bags for a field trip. Each bag has 4 apple slices and 3 grape bunches. If the school makes 5 bags, explain how you could find the total number of fruit pieces without counting them one by one. Describe two different ways to think about how the total is calculated.
2. Possible Misconceptions
1. Partial Distribution: A student might multiply the number of bags by the first item (apples) but only add the second item (grapes) once (e.g., calculating $5 \times 4 + 3$).
2. A
snack_pack_distribution.teachingPrompt: Help Zippy understand the concepts in Snack Pack Distribution.
misconceptions: Partial Distribution: A student might multiply the number of bags by the first item (apples) but only add the second item (grapes) once (e.g., calculating $5 \times 4 + 3$).
Additive Reasoning: A student might add the number of bags to the count of items in a single bag (e.g., $5 + 4 + 3$) instead of using multiplication for the groups.
Item Confusion: A student might believe they cannot add apples and grapes together to find a 'per-bag' total because the items are different types, missing the common unit of 'fruit pieces'.

sections.studentPrompt:
A school is packing snack bags for a field trip. Each bag has 4 apple slices and 3 grape bunches. If the school makes 5 bags, explain how you could find the total number of fruit pieces without counting them one by one. Describe two different ways to think about how the total is calculated.
2. Possible Misconceptions
1. Partial Distribution: A student might multiply the number of bags by the first item (apples) but only add the second item (grapes) once (e.g., calculating $5 \times 4 + 3$).
2. Additive Reasoning: A student might add the number of bags to the count of items in a single bag (e.g., $5 + 4 + 3$) instead of using multiplication for the groups.
3. Item Confusion: A student might believe they cannot add apples and grapes together to find a 'per-bag' total because the items are different types, missing the common unit of 'fruit pieces'.
misconceptions: Partial Distribution: A student might multiply the number of bags by the first item (apples) but only add the second item (grapes) once (e.g., calculating $5 \times 4 + 3$).
Additive Reasoning: A student might add the number of bags to the count of items in a single bag (e.g., $5 + 4 + 3$) instead of using multiplication for the groups.
Item Confusion: A student might believe they cannot add apples and grapes together to find a 'per-bag' total because the items are different types, missing the common unit of 'fruit pieces'.
sections.generalization: Think about any situation where you have several identical groups, and each group contains two different types of items.'Calculating the total of one group first and then multiplying by the number of groups results in the same total as multiplying each item type by the number of groups and then adding the results together.'Is this statement always, sometimes, or never true? Use your own examples or reasoning to justify your answer.
sections.inference: A store sells 'Mystery Boxes.' Each box has some toy cars and 2 stickers. We don't know how many cars are in a box, but we know every box is the same. If a customer buys 10 boxes, explain how the total number of items in their purchase would change if the store decided to put one extra sticker in every box. How can you be sure of the change even if you don't know the number of cars?

equivalent_expressions_team_jersey_order.description: A soccer coach is buying jerseys for the team. Each jersey costs a certain amount of money, but we don't know the exact price yet. The coach needs to
equivalent_expressions_team_jersey_order.problemStatement:
A soccer coach is buying jerseys for the team. Each jersey costs a certain amount of money, but we don't know the exact price yet. The coach needs to buy 3 jerseys for the goalies and 3 jerseys for the strikers.One player says the total cost is 'three times the price, plus another three times the price.'Another player says the total cost is 'six times the price.'If the price of one jersey is $10, do both players get the same total? What if the price is $20? Explain why this happens.
2. Possible
equivalent_expressions_team_jersey_order.teachingPrompt: Help Zippy understand the concepts in Equivalent Expressions Team Jersey Order.
misconceptions: Operation Confusion: A student might believe that $3p + 3p$ is $6p^2$ because they think they must multiply the variables as well as the coefficients.
Variable Misinterpretation: A student might think $3p + 3$ is the same as $3p + 3p$, failing to realize that a constant cannot be combined with a variable term.

Single-Value Equivalence: A student might think two expressions are equivalent just because they produce the same result for one specific number (like zero), without checking if they work for all values.

the_community_garden_fence.title: The Community Garden Fence

the_community_garden_fence.problemStatement:
A neighborhood is building a small community garden. They have 24 feet of decorative wood to use for the border. They are looking at several different pre-made garden frames at the store.The lengths of the frames available are: 4 feet, 6 feet, 8 feet, and 10 feet.If the garden must be a square, which of these frame lengths can the neighborhood choose so they use exactly all 24 feet of wood? 2. Possible Misconceptions

the_community_garden_fence.teachingPrompt: Help Zippy understand the concepts in The Community Garden Fence.

misconceptions: Area vs. Perimeter: A student might try to find a number that, when multiplied by itself (squared), equals 24, rather than multiplying by 4 for the four sides of a square.
Ignoring the 'Exactly' Constraint: A student might pick a length that uses less than 24 feet (like the 4-foot frame), thinking it 'fits' without realizing the equation requires equality.
Calculation Error in Substitution: A student might incorrectly substitute a value (e.g., thinking $4 imes 8 = 24$) and declare it a solution because they aren't double-checking the product.

sections.studentPrompt:
A neighborhood is building a small community garden. They have 24 feet of decorative wood to use for the border. They are looking at several different pre-made garden frames at the store.The lengths of the frames available are: 4 feet, 6 feet, 8 feet, and 10 feet.If the garden must be a square, which of these frame lengths can the neighborhood choose so they use exactly all 24 feet of wood? Explain how you know which length works and why the others do not.
2. Possible Misconceptions
1. Area vs. Perimeter: A student might try to find a number that, when multiplied by itself (squared), equals 24, rather than multiplying by 4 for the four sides of a square.
2. Ignoring the 'Exactly' Constraint: A student might pick a length that uses less than 24 feet (like the 4-foot frame), thinking it 'fits' without realizing the equation requires equality.
3. Calculation Error in Substitution: A student might incorrectly substitute a value (e.g., thinking $4 imes 8 = 24$) and declare it a solution because they aren't double-checking the product.
misconceptions: Area vs. Perimeter: A student might try to find a number that, when multiplied by itself (squared), equals 24, rather than multiplying by 4 for the four sides of a square.
Ignoring the 'Exactly' Constraint: A student might pick a length that uses less than 24 feet (like the 4-foot frame), thinking it 'fits' without realizing the equation requires equality.
Calculation Error in Substitution: A student might incorrectly substitute a value (e.g., thinking $4 imes 8 = 24$) and declare it a solution because they aren't double-checking the product.
sections.generalization: Consider this statement: 'An equation can have many different numbers as a solution, but an inequality can only ever have one correct solution.'Is this statement Always, Sometimes, or Never true? Use the garden frame examples to explain your thinking.
sections.inference: A new shipment of frames arrives with lengths of 5.5 feet, 6.1 feet, and 7 feet. Without doing the full math for every single one, predict which of these frames would make the inequality 'Total Wood ≤ 24' true. How does knowing the solution to the first question (the 6-foot frame) help you make this prediction?

the_digital_music_subscription.description: A music service charges a flat monthly fee of $10. In addition to the fee, each song you download costs a certain amount. We don't know the cost of a
the_digital_music_subscription.problemStatement:
A music service charges a flat monthly fee of $10. In addition to the fee, each song you download costs a certain amount. We don't know the cost of a song yet, but we know it's the same for every song.If a person downloads 8 songs, describe how you would calculate their total bill for the month without using a single final number. Just explain the steps you would take.
2. Possible Misconceptions
1. Misplacing the Variable: A student might attach the variable to the flat fee instead of the per-it
the_digital_music_subscription.teachingPrompt: Help Zippy understand the concepts in The Digital Music Subscription.
targetConcepts: Variables to represent numbers and write expressions
misconceptions: Misplacing the Variable: A student might attach the variable to the flat fee instead of the per-item cost (e.g., thinking the $10 is what changes per song).
Additive Variable: A student might think the relationship is additive (Fee + Song Cost) rather than realizing the song cost must be multiplied by the quantity of songs.
Ignoring the Fixed Cost: A student might only focus on the cost of the songs and forget to include the starting 'flat fee' in their logic.

the_greenhouse_fundraising_goal.problemStatement:
A school garden club is raising money to buy a small greenhouse. The greenhouse costs exactly $300. So far, the club has raised $120 through a bake sale.Explain how you would find out how much more money they still need to raise. Describe your steps without providing a final answer.

1. Incorrect Operation: A student might add the current amount to the goal ($300 + $120$) instead of subtracting to find the difference.
2. Multiplicative Confusion: In a multiplication sce
the_greenhouse_fundraising_goal.teachingPrompt: Help Zippy understand the concepts in The Greenhouse Fundraising Goal.
targetConcepts: Real-world and mathematical problems by writing and solving equations of the form x + p = q and px = q
misconceptions: Incorrect Operation: A student might add the current amount to the goal ($300 + $120$) instead of subtracting to find the difference.
Multiplicative Confusion: In a multiplication scenario (like px = q), a student might multiply the total by the rate (e.g., $300 \times 120$) instead of dividing to find the missing variable.

2. Possible Misconceptions
1. Inclusion of the Boundary: Thinking 'older than 13' includes the number 13 itself (confusing > with ≥).

3. Dis
the_movie_theater_age_limit.teachingPrompt: Help Zippy understand the concepts in The Movie Theater Age Limit.

misconceptions: Inclusion of the Boundary: Thinking 'older than 13' includes the number 13 itself (confusing > with ≥).

the_battery_life_mystery.title: The Battery Life Mystery
the_battery_life_mystery.standardDescription: 6.EE.C.9 - Use variables to represent two quantities that change in relationship to one another.
the_battery_life_mystery.description: A person is using a high-powered flashlight. Every hour the flashlight is left on, it uses up exactly 15% of its total battery life.If the flashlight
the_battery_life_mystery.problemStatement:
A person is using a high-powered flashlight. Every hour the flashlight is left on, it uses up exactly 15% of its total battery life.If the flashlight starts at 100% battery, how much battery life is left after 1 hour? How much is left after 2 hours? Describe what is happening to the battery percentage as the time increases.
2. Possible Misconceptions
1. Dependent/Independent Swap: Thinking that the battery level determines how much time has passed, rather than the time passed determining the bat
the_battery_life_mystery.teachingPrompt: Help Zippy understand the concepts in The Battery Life Mystery.
targetConcepts: Variables to represent two quantities that change in relationship to one another
misconceptions: Dependent/Independent Swap: Thinking that the battery level determines how much time has passed, rather than the time passed determining the battery level.
Additive instead of Subtractive: Assuming the battery life increases by 15% each hour (starting from 100% and going to 115%).
Rate Application Error: Applying 15% to the *current* battery level each time (exponential decay) instead of the *original* total (linear), which is common when students see percentages.

the_custom_patio_design.problemStatement:
A homeowner is planning a rectangular patio that is 8 feet long and 6 feet wide. Describe how you could determine the total amount of stone needed to cover the surface of the patio without counting every single square foot.
2. Possible Misconceptions
1. Perimeter Confusion: A student might add the lengths of all four sides ($8+6+8+6$) instead of multiplying, confusing the boundary with the surface area.
2. Slant Height Error: In shapes like parallelograms or triangles, a student might use the le
the_custom_patio_design.teachingPrompt: Help Zippy understand the concepts in The Custom Patio Design.
misconceptions: Perimeter Confusion: A student might add the lengths of all four sides ($8+6+8+6$) instead of multiplying, confusing the boundary with the surface area.
Slant Height Error: In shapes like parallelograms or triangles, a student might use the length of a slanted side instead of the vertical height to calculate area.
Dimension Overload: A student might multiply all four side lengths together ($8 \times 6 \times 8 \times 6$) believing that every side must be included in an area calculation.

the_sandbox_filling_challenge.problemStatement:
A small sandbox is shaped like a rectangular box. It is 4 feet long, 3 feet wide, and 1 foot deep.Describe how you could find out how many 1-foot cubes of sand would be needed to fill the sandbox completely without counting them one by one.
2. Possible Misconceptions
1. Area vs. Volume: A student might multiply only the length and width (4 × 3 = 12), forgetting to account for the depth/height of the box.
2. Additive Reasoning: A student might add the dimensions ($4 + 3 + 1$) instead of using mul
the_sandbox_filling_challenge.teachingPrompt: Help Zippy understand the concepts in The Sandbox Filling Challenge.
targetConcepts: The volume of a right rectangular prism with fractional edge lengths
misconceptions: Area vs. Volume: A student might multiply only the length and width (4 × 3 = 12), forgetting to account for the depth/height of the box.
Additive Reasoning: A student might add the dimensions ($4 + 3 + 1$) instead of using multiplication to find the total capacity.

Fractional Misalignment: When dimensions are fractions (like 1/2 foot), a student might think they need fewer cubes than for a whole foot, failing to realize that smaller 'unit cubes' (like 1/2-ft cubes) would require more units to fill the same space.

the_shipping_box_designer.title: The Shipping Box Designer
the_shipping_box_designer.standardDescription: 6.G.A.4 - Represent three-dimensional figures using nets made up of rectangles and triangles, and use the nets to find the surface area of these figures.
the_shipping_box_designer.description: A company is designing a simple cardboard box. The box is a cube where every side is 5 inches long. If you were to cut the box along some of its edges
the_shipping_box_designer.problemStatement:
A company is designing a simple cardboard box. The box is a cube where every side is 5 inches long. If you were to cut the box along some of its edges and flatten it out completely on a table, describe what the flat shape would look like. misconceptions: Inverting the Wrong Fraction: A student might multiply 1/4 by 1/2 instead of 4 by 2, or incorrectly invert the dividend instead of the divisor.

the_farmers_market_booth.problemStatement:
A farmer is selling honey at the market. One large jar of honey costs $12.50. A customer buys two jars and pays with a $50 bill.Describe the steps you would take to find out how much change the customer should receive. Explain your thinking without providing the final answer.
2. Possible Misconceptions
1. Misalignment of Place Value: When subtracting $12.50$ from $50$, a student might line up the '5' in 50 with the '5' in 0.50, failing to treat 50 as 50.00.
2. Ignoring the Decimal in Multiplicat
the_farmers_market_booth.teachingPrompt: Help Zippy understand the concepts in The Farmer's Market Booth.
misconceptions: Misalignment of Place Value: When subtracting $12.50$ from $50$, a student might line up the '5' in 50 with the '5' in 0.50, failing to treat 50 as 50.00.
Ignoring the Decimal in Multiplication: A student might multiply $1250 \times 2$ and forget to place the decimal point back into the product, resulting in $2500$ instead of $25.00$.

Shifted Divisor in Division: If dividing a total by a decimal (e.g., $15 \div 0.75$), a student might forget to move the decimal point in both the divisor and the dividend, leading to a quotient that is off by a factor of 10 or 100.

the_community_gift_basket.title: The Community Gift Basket
the_community_gift_basket.standardDescription: 6.NS.B.4 - Find the greatest common factor and least common multiple; use the distributive property to express a sum of two whole numbers.

the_community_gift_basket.problemStatement:
A volunteer group is making identical gift baskets for a local shelter. They have 18 jars of jam and 24 boxes of tea. They want to use all the items, and every basket must have the exact same number of jam jars and the exact same number of tea boxes.If they decide to make only 2 gift baskets, how many of each item would go into each basket? What if they wanted to make as many baskets as possible? Explain how you would figure that out.
2. Possible Misconceptions
1. GCF vs. LCM Confusion: A studen
the_community_gift_basket.teachingPrompt: Help Zippy understand the concepts in The Community Gift Basket.
targetConcepts: The greatest common factor and least common multiple
The distributive property to express a sum of two whole numbers
misconceptions: GCF vs. LCM Confusion: A student might try to find the Least Common Multiple (72) instead of the Greatest Common Factor (6), leading them to think they need 72 baskets.
Remainder Neglect: A student might pick a number that is a factor of one item but not the other (e.g., trying to make 4 baskets), resulting in leftover items.

Misinterpreting the Distributive Property: When rewriting $18 + 24$, a student might factor out a common factor but fail to divide both terms correctly (e.g., writing $2(9 + 24)$ instead of $2(9 + 12)$).

misconceptions: Magnitude Confusion: Thinking that -10 is 'larger' than 5 because the digit 10 is bigger than 5, failing to recognize that negative numbers further from zero represent lower values.

the_treasure_map_coordinates.problemStatement:
Imagine a giant number line drawn on the playground. You are standing at 0. If you walk 4 steps to the right, you reach the number 4. If you walk 4 steps to the left of 0, what number are you standing on? How can you describe your position to someone who can't see you, so they know exactly which side of 0 you are on?
2. Possible Misconceptions
1. Mirror Confusion: A student might think that the number to the left of 0 is still '4' because they are only counting the distance (magnitude) and ignor
the_treasure_map_coordinates.teachingPrompt: Help Zippy understand the concepts in The Treasure Map Coordinates.
targetConcepts: A rational number as a point on the number line
Points on the line and in the plane with negative number coordinates
misconceptions: Mirror Confusion: A student might think that the number to the left of 0 is still '4' because they are only counting the distance (magnitude) and ignoring the direction (sign).
Quadrant Reversal: When moving to a coordinate plane, a student might think $(-3, 2)$ is the same as $(3, -2)$, failing to realize that the order of x and y determines the specific quadrant.
Fractional Placement: A student might place $-1.5$ between $0$ and $-1$, thinking that because $1.5$ is 'bigger' than $1$, it must be closer to zero on the negative side.

misconceptions: Subtraction Error across Quadrants: A student might subtract the absolute values (5 - 3 = 2) instead of adding them (3 + 5 = 8), failing to realize that crossing an axis requires adding distances from zero.

Diagonal Thinking: Thinking the driver can take a shortcut diagonally, which would require the Pythagorean theorem, rather than following the grid-based 'Manhattan distance' required by the standard.

sections.studentPrompt:
A delivery driver uses a coordinate grid to navigate a neighborhood. The local Bakery is located at (4, 3) and the Grocery Store is located at (4, -5).If the driver travels in a straight line from the Bakery to the Grocery Store, which direction are they traveling? Describe how you would find the total distance of this trip without counting every single square on the grid.
2. Possible Misconceptions
1. Subtraction Error across Quadrants: A student might subtract the absolute values (5 - 3 = 2) instead of adding them (3 + 5 = 8), failing to realize that crossing an axis requires adding distances from zero.
2. Coordinate Swap: Attempting to find the distance between the x-values (4 and 4) which results in zero, rather than focusing on the changing y-values.
3. Diagonal Thinking: Thinking the driver can take a shortcut diagonally, which would require the Pythagorean theorem, rather than following the grid-based 'Manhattan distance' required by the standard.

Explain how you can be sure of your answer using what you know about distance and absolute value.

misconceptions: Additive vs. Multiplicative: A student might describe the relationship as 'there are 4 more students than adults' (additive) instead of recognizing the 3:1 ratio (multiplicative).

misconceptions: Additive Reasoning: A student might think that since the blue paint increased by 3 cups (6 - 3 = 3), the red paint should also increase by 3 cups (2 + 3 = 5), resulting in a different shade of purple.

the_school_cafeteria_survey.problemStatement:
A student is curious about lunch at school. They come up with two different questions to ask:Question A: 'What did the principal eat for lunch today?'Question B: 'What did the students in the 6th grade eat for lunch today?'If you asked 20 different people Question A, how many different answers would you expect to get? If you asked 20 different people Question B, would you expect all the answers to be the same? Why or why not?
2. Possible Misconceptions
1. Topic vs. Structure: A student might thi
the_school_cafeteria_survey.teachingPrompt: Help Zippy understand the concepts in The School Cafeteria Survey.
targetConcepts: A statistical question as one that anticipates variability in the data related to the question and accounts for it in the answers
misconceptions: Topic vs. Structure: A student might think a question is statistical just because it is about a 'mathy' topic (like height or grades), even if the question only has one fixed answer (e.g., 'How tall is the teacher?').
Variability as Error: A student might view different answers as 'wrong' or 'mistakes' rather than a natural spread of data (variability).

Sample Size Confusion: Thinking a question only becomes statistical if you ask a lot of people, rather than recognizing that the nature of the question itself must allow for multiple possible answers.

1. Measure Confusion: Thinking that the 'variation' (spread) is just another way t
the_weekend_weather_report.teachingPrompt: Help Zippy understand the concepts in The Weekend Weather Report.
targetConcepts: That a measure of center for a numerical data set summarizes all of its values with a single number
misconceptions: Measure Confusion: Thinking that the 'variation' (spread) is just another way to find the 'center' (average), rather than understanding they describe two different characteristics of the data.
Variation as Inaccuracy: Believing that a high measure of variation (like in City 2) means the data is 'wrong' or unreliable, rather than simply being diverse.
Single-Number Sufficiency: Assuming that if two cities have the same mean, their weather patterns are identical, ignoring the impact of extremes (very hot or very cold days).

1. Outlier Over-influence: A student might calculate the mean and find it is 12 lbs, but fail to notice that the 25-lb 'giant' watermelon is pulling the average away from the most common cluster (9-12 lbs).
2. Ignoring Context: A student might provide a measure of center (like the median) without units or without explaining what it means for the garden (e.g., 'The answer is 11' vs. 'Most watermelons are about 11 lbs').
3. Mean vs. Median Appropriateness: Thinking the mean is always the 'best' summary, even when a data set is heavily skewed by a single unusual value.

snack_stand_expressions.title: Snack Stand Expressions
snack_stand_expressions.description: A snack stand sells "Game Day Packs." Each pack contains 1 bag of popcorn and 2 juice boxes. Imagine a customer walks up and buys several of these pac
snack_stand_expressions.problemStatement:
A snack stand sells "Game Day Packs." Each pack contains 1 bag of popcorn and 2 juice boxes. Imagine a customer walks up and buys several of these packs. Describe what is inside their bag and how you know what the contents are.
2. Possible Misconceptions
The following misconceptions could be addressed in later prompts:
• The "One-Time Addition" Error: A student believes that no matter how many packs are bought, you only add 2 juice boxes once (e.g., for 5 packs, they calculate 5 + 2 = 7 items).
snack_stand_expressions.teachingPrompt: Help Zippy understand the concepts in Snack Stand Expressions.
misconceptions: The following misconceptions could be addressed in later prompts:
• The "One-Time Addition" Error: A student believes that no matter how many packs are bought, you only add 2 juice boxes once (e.g., for 5 packs, they calculate 5 + 2 = 7 items).

• The "Type vs. Quantity" Error: A student thinks there are only 3 items total in the bag because they only count the types of items mentioned (1 popcorn and 2 juices), regardless of the number of packs.
• The "Fixed Total" Error: A student assumes there is a specific number of packs intended (like 1 pack) and cannot describe the contents without being given a specific number of packs first.

harvest_packing_challenge.description: A local farm has 4,320 apples to pack for a community market. Each crate holds 18 apples. How many crates will be completely filled?
harvest_packing_challenge.problemStatement:
A local farm has 4,320 apples to pack for a community market. Each crate holds 18 apples. How many crates will be completely filled?
2. Potential Misconceptions
Later prompts will address these common errors:
Place Value Omission: Forgetting to record a zero in the quotient when the divisor does not fit into a brought-down digit (e.g., getting 24 instead of 240).
Estimation Ignorance: Not checking if the answer is 'reasonable' (e.g., realizing that 24 crates of 18 is far less than 4,000).

6-ee-a-2-c-the-backyard-architect.title: The Backyard Architect
6-ee-a-2-c-the-backyard-architect.standardDescription: Evaluate expressions at specific values of their variables. Include expressions that arise from formulas used in real-world problems. Perform arithmetic operations, including those involving whole-number exponents, in the conventional order when there are no parentheses to specify a particular order (Order of Operations).
6-ee-a-2-c-the-backyard-architect.description: I'm helping my aunt design a square fire pit and a rectangular patio
6-ee-a-2-c-the-backyard-architect.problemStatement: "I'm helping my aunt design a square fire pit and a rectangular patio. She gave me this formula for the total cost of materials: $C = 5s^2 + 2(L + W)$. She says $s$ is the side of the square pit, and $L$ and $W$ are the length and width of the patio. If $s = 3$ feet, $L = 10$ feet, and $W = 8$ feet, how do I solve this? My friend says I should multiply 5 by 3 and *then* square it, but I think the exponent only 'touches' the 3. If I do it in the wrong order, am I going to end up with a bill for \$500 or \$100?"
6-ee-a-2-c-the-backyard-architect.teachingPrompt: Help Zippy understand The Backyard Architect by teaching the key mathematical ideas step by step.
misconceptions: Exponent Precedence: Students often multiply the coefficient by the base before applying the exponent (e.g., calculating $5 \times 3 = 15$, then $15^2 = 225$). They need to recognize that exponents ($E$) come before multiplication ($M$) in the Order of Operations.
Parentheses "Distribution" Errors: A student might multiply $2 \times 10$ but forget to multiply the 2 by the 8, or they might add $2 + 10$ before finishing the work inside the parentheses.

Left-to-Right Rigidity: Students may simply solve the expression from left to right as if reading a sentence, ignoring the hierarchy of operations entirely (e.g., doing $5s^2 + 2$ then multiplying the whole thing by $L+W$).
6-ee-a-2-c-the-backyard-architect.aiIntro: Hi! I'm Zippy! I need help with "The Backyard Architect". "I'm helping my aunt design a square fire pit and a rectangular patio. She gave me this formula for the total cost of materials: $C = 5s^2 + 2(L + W)$. I was thinking about it and got confused because exponent precedence. Can you help me understand?

She says $s$ is the side of the square pit, and $L$ and $W$ are the length and width of the patio. If $s = 3$ feet, $L = 10$ feet, and $W = 8$ feet, how do I solve this? My friend says I should multiply 5 by 3 and *then* square it, but I think the exponent only 'touches' the 3. If I do it in the wrong order, am I going to end up with a bill for \$500 or \$100?"
misconceptions: Exponent Precedence: Students often multiply the coefficient by the base before applying the exponent (e.g., calculating $5 \times 3 = 15$, then $15^2 = 225$). They need to recognize that exponents ($E$) come before multiplication ($M$) in the Order of Operations.
Parentheses "Distribution" Errors: A student might multiply $2 \times 10$ but forget to multiply the 2 by the 8, or they might add $2 + 10$ before finishing the work inside the parentheses.

Left-to-Right Rigidity: Students may simply solve the expression from left to right as if reading a sentence, ignoring the hierarchy of operations entirely (e.g., doing $5s^2 + 2$ then multiplying the whole thing by $L+W$).
sections.patternRecognition:
- "If the area of a square is $s \times s$, and you have 5 of those squares, do you find the area of one square first or multiply the side by 5 first?"
- "Wait—if you have $2(10 + 8)$, does it make more sense to find the total 'package' inside the parentheses first, or try to break in from the outside?"
- "In the expression $5 \times 3^2$, if the '2' is a tiny hat on the '3', who gets to go to the party first: the hat or the neighbor (the 5)?"
sections.generalization:
Question (Always / Sometimes / Never)
"I have a hunch that if you have an expression with both exponents and multiplication, the exponent part will ALWAYS result in a much bigger change to your final answer than the multiplication part... or is there a case where multiplication 'wins'?"
sections.inference:
"We decided to use a different formula for the stone border: $B = (2s)^2$. If $s$ is still 3, will this give us the same answer as our original $5s^2$ part? How does moving the parentheses to include the 2 change the 'power' of that exponent?"

**Prediction target:** Recognizing that $(2 \times 3)^2 = 36$, while $2 \times 3^2 = 18$. Identifying that parentheses regroup the order of operations.

claims: Students can substitute specific values into algebraic expressions and formulas.
Students can evaluate expressions involving exponents, parentheses, and multiple operations using the standard Order of Operations.
mappingData.evidence: Evidence is gathered when a student correctly evaluates $5(3^2)$ as $5 \times 9 = 45$ instead of $15^2 = 225$. The task forces this by using a real-world "cost" context where the magnitude of the error ($225$ vs $45$) is high enough to prompt the student to double-check their logic.
mappingData.criticalThinking: A student demonstrates deep understanding when they can articulate that the exponent applies only to the base it is attached to unless parentheses tell them otherwise. Self-correction is evident when a student re-evaluates the $2(10 + 8)$ term and realizes that adding the lengths before doubling represents the perimeter correctly.

6-ee-a-2-b-the-expression-anatomy-lab.title: The Expression Anatomy Lab
6-ee-a-2-b-the-expression-anatomy-lab.standardDescription: Identify parts of an expression using mathematical terms (sum, term, product, factor, quotient, coefficient); view one or more parts of an expression as a single entity. For example, describe the expression 2(8 + 7) as a product of two factors; view (8 + 7) as both a single entity and a sum of two terms.
6-ee-a-2-b-the-expression-anatomy-lab.description: I’m looking at this long math sentence: [math]
6-ee-a-2-b-the-expression-anatomy-lab.problemStatement: "I’m looking at this long math sentence: $3(x + 5) + 4y - 9$. My teacher says it has 'terms,' 'coefficients,' and 'factors,' but it looks like a big jumble to me! She also said that even though $(x + 5)$ has two numbers inside, I should sometimes look at the whole parenthesis as just one single 'thing.' How can one part of a problem be a 'sum' and a 'factor' at the same time? And which numbers are the ones actually 'bossing around' the variables?"
6-ee-a-2-b-the-expression-anatomy-lab.teachingPrompt: Help Zippy understand The Expression Anatomy Lab by teaching the key mathematical ideas step by step.
misconceptions: Term Count Confusion: Students often count every single number and letter as a "term." They might say $4y$ is two terms because there are two characters, failing to see that terms are separated by plus and minus signs ($4y$ is a single term).

Coefficient vs. Constant: A student might call the $9$ a coefficient because it's a number, or call the $3$ a constant, missing the functional difference: coefficients "co-operate" with variables (multiplication), while constants stand alone.
Parentheses as "Invisible": When looking at $3(x + 5)$, a student might only see the 3 as a factor, failing to recognize that the *entire sum* $(x + 5)$ is the second factor in that multiplication.

My teacher says it has 'terms,' 'coefficients,' and 'factors,' but it looks like a big jumble to me! She also said that even though $(x + 5)$ has two numbers inside, I should sometimes look at the whole parenthesis as just one single 'thing. I was thinking about it and got confused because term count confusion. Can you help me understand?

She also said that even though $(x + 5)$ has two numbers inside, I should sometimes look at the whole parenthesis as just one single 'thing.' How can one part of a problem be a 'sum' and a 'factor' at the same time? And which numbers are the ones actually 'bossing around' the variables?"
misconceptions: Term Count Confusion: Students often count every single number and letter as a "term." They might say $4y$ is two terms because there are two characters, failing to see that terms are separated by plus and minus signs ($4y$ is a single term).

Coefficient vs. Constant: A student might call the $9$ a coefficient because it's a number, or call the $3$ a constant, missing the functional difference: coefficients "co-operate" with variables (multiplication), while constants stand alone.
Parentheses as "Invisible": When looking at $3(x + 5)$, a student might only see the 3 as a factor, failing to recognize that the *entire sum* $(x + 5)$ is the second factor in that multiplication.
sections.patternRecognition:
- "If you cover $(x + 5)$ with a sticky note, the expression looks like $3(\square) + 4y - 9$. Does that help you see the three main 'chunks' or terms separated by the $+$ and $-$?"
- "Wait—in the term $4y$, what is the 'co-pilot' (coefficient) helping the variable $y$ grow?"
- "If factors are things that are multiplied, and we are multiplying 3 by $(x + 5)$, can we call that whole parenthesis a single factor?"
sections.generalization:
Question (Always / Sometimes / Never)
"I have a hunch that a 'coefficient' is ALWAYS a number attached to a letter, while a 'constant' is ALWAYS a number sitting by itself... or is there a case where a number can be both?"
sections.inference:
"I found a new expression: $\frac{w + 10}{2}$. If we treat the top part ($w + 10$) as a single entity, would you call this a 'product' or a 'quotient'? And if we rewrote it as $\frac{1}{2}(w + 10)$, how does the 'anatomy' of the expression change? Does $(w + 10)$ go from being a 'dividend' to being a 'factor'?"

**Prediction target:** Recognizing that the same quantity can change its "role" (dividend vs. factor) depending on how the expression is structured.

claims: Students can identify terms, coefficients, constants, and factors within a complex expression.
Students can describe a sub-expression (like a sum in parentheses) as a single entity within a larger product.
mappingData.evidence: Evidence is gathered when a student identifies that $3(x + 5) + 4y - 9$ has three terms and that $(x + 5)$ acts as a single factor. The task forces this by using parentheses, which physically group "two things" into "one entity," requiring the student to shift their perspective.
mappingData.criticalThinking: A student demonstrates deep understanding when they can explain that the coefficient (like 4 in $4y$) describes the *rate* or "how many groups," while the constant (like -9) is a fixed starting or ending point. Self-correction occurs when a student realizes that $3$ is not just a number, but a "factor" of the entire quantity $(x + 5)$.

8-ee-a-1-the-doubling-dilemma.title: The Doubling Dilemma
8-ee-a-1-the-doubling-dilemma.standardDescription: Know and apply the properties of integer exponents to generate equivalent numerical expressions.
8-ee-a-1-the-doubling-dilemma.description: I was looking at this pattern where a cell doubles every hour, so after 3 hours there are [math] cells, and I wondered w
8-ee-a-1-the-doubling-dilemma.problemStatement: I was looking at this pattern where a cell doubles every hour, so after 3 hours there are $2^3$ cells, and I wondered what happens if you multiply that by another $2^2$ cells. Does that mean you have $4^5$ cells now because you doubled the bottom and added the top, or is that not how it works?
8-ee-a-1-the-doubling-dilemma.teachingPrompt: Help Zippy understand The Doubling Dilemma by teaching the key mathematical ideas step by step.
misconceptions: Base Multiplier Error: The student multiplies the bases together (2 times 2) while also adding the exponents, resulting in $4^5$ instead of $2^5$.
Exponent Multiplication: The student multiplies the exponents (3 times 2) instead of adding them, leading to an answer of $2^6$.
Linear Addition Bias: The student treats the entire expression as a simple addition problem ($2^3 + 2^2$) rather than recognizing the multiplicative nature of exponential growth.
8-ee-a-1-the-doubling-dilemma.aiIntro: Hi! I'm Zippy! I need help with "The Doubling Dilemma". I was looking at this pattern where a cell doubles every hour, so after 3 hours there are $2^3$ cells, and I wondered what happens if you multiply that by another $2^2$ cells. Does that mean you have $4^5$ cells now because you doubled the bottom and added the top, or is that not how it works?. I was thinking about it and got confused because base multiplier error. Can you help me understand?

sections.studentPrompt: I was looking at this pattern where a cell doubles every hour, so after 3 hours there are $2^3$ cells, and I wondered what happens if you multiply that by another $2^2$ cells. Does that mean you have $4^5$ cells now because you doubled the bottom and added the top, or is that not how it works?
misconceptions: Base Multiplier Error: The student multiplies the bases together (2 times 2) while also adding the exponents, resulting in $4^5$ instead of $2^5$.
Exponent Multiplication: The student multiplies the exponents (3 times 2) instead of adding them, leading to an answer of $2^6$.
Linear Addition Bias: The student treats the entire expression as a simple addition problem ($2^3 + 2^2$) rather than recognizing the multiplicative nature of exponential growth.
sections.patternRecognition:
- If you wrote out all the "2s" being multiplied in $2^3$ and then wrote out all the "2s" in $2^2$ right next to them, what do you notice about the total count?
- When the number of hours changes, does the "doubling" part of the math ever actually turn into "quadrupling"?
- How does the total number of "2s" in the long string connect back to the little numbers at the top?
sections.generalization:
Question (Always / Sometimes / Never)
"Wait, so if the big numbers at the bottom are the same, is it ALWAYS true that you can just add the tiny numbers at the top to find the total, even if the exponents are negative?"
sections.inference:
I found this other weird case where a scientist had $2^8$ bacteria but then it got divided by $2^3$ because of some medicine. If multiplying them made the exponent bigger, do you think dividing them just makes the exponent smaller in the same way?

**Prediction target:** The resulting exponent when $2^8$ is divided by $2^3$, and whether the base stays a 2.

claims: The student can apply the Product of Powers property ($a^m \cdot a^n = a^{m+n}$) to generate equivalent expressions.
The student can justify why the base remains constant when multiplying powers with like bases.
mappingData.evidence: The task requires students to expand the exponential notation into repeated multiplication to verify their logic. Deep reasoning is evidenced when a student explains that multiplying $2^3$ by $2^2$ is simply a string of five 2s, which forces the realization that the base cannot become a 4.
mappingData.criticalThinking: An observable behavior of deep understanding is when a student self-corrects from $4^5$ to $2^5$ by physically or mentally counting the factors and realizing that "doubling" is the constant rule regardless of how many groups are combined.

6-ee-a-the-mystery-number-machine.title: The Mystery Number Machine
6-ee-a-the-mystery-number-machine.standardDescription: Apply and extend previous understandings of arithmetic to algebraic expressions (including exponents, variables, and identifying equivalent expressions).
6-ee-a-the-mystery-number-machine.description: I found a 'Number Machine' in the basement
6-ee-a-the-mystery-number-machine.problemStatement: "I found a 'Number Machine' in the basement. Every time I put a number $n$ in, it doubles it and then adds 5. My friend says I can write this as $2n + 5$, but my cousin says it's $(n + n) + 5$, and my teacher says it's also $n + 5 + n$. I’m confused—how can three different-looking math sentences all describe the exact same machine? And what happens if I decide to put a 'square' on the number, like $n^2$? Does that just mean the number times 2, or is it something totally different?"
6-ee-a-the-mystery-number-machine.teachingPrompt: Help Zippy understand The Mystery Number Machine by teaching the key mathematical ideas step by step.
misconceptions: Exponent Confusion: Students often think $n^2$ means $n \times 2$. They struggle to realize that an exponent is a shorthand for repeated multiplication ($n \times n$), not a doubling instruction.

Variable Juxtaposition: A student might see $2n$ and think it means "twenty-something" (like if $n=3$, they see 23) instead of understanding that a number next to a variable implies multiplication ($2 \times n$).
Order of Operations (PEMDAS) Drift: When evaluating $2n + 5$ for $n=3$, a student might add $3+5$ first to get 8, then multiply by 2 to get 16, rather than following the hierarchy of operations.
6-ee-a-the-mystery-number-machine.aiIntro: Hi! I'm Zippy! I need help with "The Mystery Number Machine". "I found a 'Number Machine' in the basement. Every time I put a number $n$ in, it doubles it and then adds 5. I was thinking about it and got confused because exponent confusion. Can you help me understand?
