sections.patternRecognition:
- "If you look at the daily sales on a dot plot, does that 500-cupcake day sit near the others or is it off on its own island?"
- "Wait—if the baker actually made 110 cupcakes every single day like the average says, what would happen to the leftovers on a normal Tuesday?"
- "Does the 'middle' of the data move more when you change the highest number, or does it stay put if you use the median instead?"
sections.generalization:
Question (Always / Sometimes / Never)
"I have this hunch that if a data set has one giant 'outlier' that's way bigger than everything else, the median is ALWAYS going to give you a better picture of a 'normal' day than the mean... or is there a case where the mean still wins?"
sections.inference:
"I found the data for a different shop that sells fancy cakes, and their sales are almost always 5, 6, or 7 cakes a day, with no huge wedding orders at all. Since their data is basically a perfect, even clump without any 'islands' far away, do you think it even matters if they use the mean or the median to describe their typical day? And like, if they DID suddenly get a massive order for 100 cakes, which measure would change the most?"

**Prediction target:** The stability of the mean vs. median in a symmetric distribution versus a skewed distribution.

claims: Students can justify the choice between mean and median based on the presence of outliers and the shape of the distribution.
Students can explain how the context of a problem (e.g., planning inventory) dictates which measure of center is most useful.
mappingData.evidence: Evidence is provided when a student identifies that the mean is "pulled" by the outlier of 500, making it an unreliable predictor for a typical day. The task design forces this reasoning by contrasting a mathematical calculation (the mean) with a practical consequence (having too many leftover cupcakes).
mappingData.criticalThinking: A student demonstrates deep understanding when they observe that the mean and median are similar in symmetric data but diverge in skewed data. Self-correction occurs when a student realizes that "mathematically correct" (calculating the mean) is not the same as "contextually appropriate" for the bakery's needs.

6-sp-b-5-the-final-data-report.title: The Final Data Report
6-sp-b-5-the-final-data-report.standardDescription: Summarize numerical data sets in relation to their context, such as by: reporting the number of observations; describing the nature of the attribute under investigation, including how it was measured and its units of measurement; giving quantitative measures of center (median and/or mean) and variability (interquartile range and/or mean absolute deviation), as well as describing any overall pattern and any striking deviations from the overall pattern with reference to the context in which the data were gathered; relating the choice of measures of center and variability to the shape of the data distribution and the context in which the data were gathered.
6-sp-b-5-the-final-data-report.description: I just finished my big science project on how many minutes of 'screen time' my classmates have each day
6-sp-b-5-the-final-data-report.problemStatement:
"I just finished my big science project on how many minutes of 'screen time' my classmates have each day. I surveyed 30 people (my **observations**). Most kids had between 60 and 90 minutes, but one person had 400 minutes! My teacher says I can't just give her a list of numbers; I have to write a summary that explains the 'story' of the data. 

If I use the **Mean**, that 400-minute outlier makes it look like *everyone* watches way more than they actually do. Should I use the **Median** instead? And how do I explain the 'variability' using the **IQR** so people know if we are all similar or all totally different?"
6-sp-b-5-the-final-data-report.teachingPrompt: Help Zippy understand The Final Data Report by teaching the key mathematical ideas step by step.
misconceptions: The 'One Size Fits All' Average: Students often believe the Mean is the "best" or "most mathematical" average. They struggle to recognize that the **Median** is actually a more "honest" measure of center when data is **skewed** by an outlier (like the 400-minute student).

Ignoring Units and Context: A student might report the number "75" without saying "minutes." They often forget to describe *how* the data was collected (e.g., "self-reported via survey") or what the "attribute" is (e.g., "daily screen time").

Confusing IQR with the Range: When describing variability, students often just give the Range (Max minus Min). They miss that the **Interquartile Range (IQR)** is better for describing the "typical" spread because it ignores those extreme outliers that don't represent the rest of the group.
6-sp-b-5-the-final-data-report.aiIntro: Hi! I'm Zippy! I need help with "The Final Data Report". "I just finished my big science project on how many minutes of 'screen time' my classmates have each day. I surveyed 30 people (my **observations**). I was thinking about it and got confused because the 'one size fits all' average. Can you help me understand?

sections.studentPrompt:
"I just finished my big science project on how many minutes of 'screen time' my classmates have each day. I surveyed 30 people (my **observations**). Most kids had between 60 and 90 minutes, but one person had 400 minutes! My teacher says I can't just give her a list of numbers; I have to write a summary that explains the 'story' of the data. 

If I use the **Mean**, that 400-minute outlier makes it look like *everyone* watches way more than they actually do. Should I use the **Median** instead? And how do I explain the 'variability' using the **IQR** so people know if we are all similar or all totally different?"
misconceptions: The 'One Size Fits All' Average: Students often believe the Mean is the "best" or "most mathematical" average. They struggle to recognize that the **Median** is actually a more "honest" measure of center when data is **skewed** by an outlier (like the 400-minute student).

Ignoring Units and Context: A student might report the number "75" without saying "minutes." They often forget to describe *how* the data was collected (e.g., "self-reported via survey") or what the "attribute" is (e.g., "daily screen time").

Confusing IQR with the Range: When describing variability, students often just give the Range (Max minus Min). They miss that the **Interquartile Range (IQR)** is better for describing the "typical" spread because it ignores those extreme outliers that don't represent the rest of the group.
sections.patternRecognition:
- "If you tell a parent the 'average' is 120 minutes because of one outlier, but most kids only watched 60, is your math helping them understand the class, or confusing them?"
- "Wait—if the 'middle 50%' of your class (the IQR) is only 15 minutes apart, does that mean most of your friends have very similar habits?"
- "When you see a 'tail' on your graph stretching toward 400, does that 'pull' the Mean away from the 'hill' where everyone else is?"
sections.generalization:
Question (Always / Sometimes / Never)
"I have a hunch that the Median and IQR are ALWAYS better than the Mean and MAD when you have a 'striking deviation' (an outlier)... or is there a reason we'd still want the Mean?"
sections.inference:
"Imagine you did the same survey at a 'No-Tech Summer Camp.' You expect 29 people to say '0 minutes' and one person to sneak in '60 minutes.' How would the **shape** of this data change? Would you still use the Median to describe it, or would a 'Mean' of 2 minutes actually be a good summary of the total screen time used by the whole camp?"

**Prediction target:** Identifying that in a very "tight" distribution with almost no variability, the Mean and Median will be nearly identical, but the context (total usage vs. typical student) still dictates which measure is more useful.

claims: Students can summarize a data set by reporting observations, units, and measurement methods.
Students can calculate and report measures of center (mean, median) and variability (IQR, MAD).
Students can justify why a specific measure (e.g., median) is more appropriate for a skewed distribution.
mappingData.evidence: Evidence is gathered when a student writes a summary that notes 30 observations, uses "minutes" as units, and explicitly chooses the Median because the 400-minute outlier skews the Mean. The task forces this by providing a "striking deviation" (the 400-minute outlier), requiring a choice between statistical measures.
mappingData.criticalThinking: A student demonstrates deep understanding when they can relate the "shape" of the data (skewed) to the "choice of measure" (median). Self-correction occurs when a student calculates the Mean, realizes it is much higher than most of the data points, and decides that the Median provides a more accurate "typical" value for the context.

6-ns-c-7-c-the-bank-balance-blur.title: The Bank Balance Blur
6-ns-c-7-c-the-bank-balance-blur.standardDescription: Understand the absolute value of a rational number as its distance from 0 on the number line; interpret absolute value as magnitude for a positive or negative quantity in a real-world situation. For example, for an account balance of -30 dollars, write |-30| = 30 to describe the size of the debt in dollars.
6-ns-c-7-c-the-bank-balance-blur.description: I looked at my bank account app and it says my balance is [math]40
6-ns-c-7-c-the-bank-balance-blur.problemStatement: "I looked at my bank account app and it says my balance is $-\$40.00$. My older brother says, 'Whoa, you have a 40-dollar debt!' but I told him he was wrong because the number is negative, so it's 'less than zero.' He says the 'size' of my problem is still 40. If the math symbol $|-40|$ just turns the number into a regular 40, does that mean the negative sign just disappears, or is it telling us something else about how far I am from having an empty wallet?"
6-ns-c-7-c-the-bank-balance-blur.teachingPrompt: Help Zippy understand The Bank Balance Blur by teaching the key mathematical ideas step by step.
misconceptions: The "Make it Positive" Magic Trick: Students often view absolute value as a "button" that just removes negative signs without understanding that it measures **distance**. This leads to confusion when they see $|40|$ and try to change it to $-40$, thinking the symbol just "switches" signs.

Value vs. Magnitude: A student might think that because $-40 < -10$, the "absolute value" of $-40$ should also be smaller. They struggle to separate the *location* of the number (value) from its *intensity* or *size* (magnitude).

Distance as Negative: A student might try to say a distance is $-40$ feet, not realizing that distance (how far you traveled) is always a positive count, even if you are traveling in a negative direction.

If the math symbol $|-40|$ just turns the number into a regular 40, does that mean the negative sign just disappears, or is it telling us something else about how far I am from having an empty wallet?"
misconceptions: The "Make it Positive" Magic Trick: Students often view absolute value as a "button" that just removes negative signs without understanding that it measures **distance**. This leads to confusion when they see $|40|$ and try to change it to $-40$, thinking the symbol just "switches" signs.

Value vs. Magnitude: A student might think that because $-40 < -10$, the "absolute value" of $-40$ should also be smaller. They struggle to separate the *location* of the number (value) from its *intensity* or *size* (magnitude).

Distance as Negative: A student might try to say a distance is $-40$ feet, not realizing that distance (how far you traveled) is always a positive count, even if you are traveling in a negative direction.
sections.patternRecognition:
- "If you walk 10 steps East, how far did you walk? If you walk 10 steps West, how far did you walk? Is the 'how far' part different?"
- "Wait—if you owe \$40 ($-\$40$) and your friend owes \$10 ($-\$10$), who has the 'bigger' debt to pay back?"
- "Does the absolute value symbol $| \text{ } |$ care about which *side* of zero you are on, or just how many *units* you are away from it?"
sections.generalization:
Question (Always / Sometimes / Never)
"I have a hunch that the absolute value of a number is ALWAYS a positive number (or zero), no matter what the original number looked like... or is there a special 'super-negative' number that stays negative even inside the bars?"
sections.inference:
"I found two divers: Diver A is at $-25$ feet and Diver B is at $-40$ feet. If we use absolute value to talk about their 'depth,' Diver B has a greater absolute value ($|-40| > |-25|$). Does having a 'greater absolute value' mean they are closer to the surface or deeper in the ocean? And what would happen to that 'magnitude' if they both swam up 10 feet?"

**Prediction target:** Identifying that larger absolute value for negative numbers means "further from zero" (deeper) and predicting the new magnitudes ($|-15| = 15$ and $|-30| = 30$).

claims: Students can define absolute value as the distance a number is from zero on a number line.
Students can interpret absolute value in real-world contexts to describe the "size" or "magnitude" of a quantity (like debt or depth).
mappingData.evidence: Evidence is provided when a student correctly identifies that $|-40| = 40$ and explains that the "40" represents the total amount of money owed, regardless of the negative sign. The task forces this by using "debt," where the negative sign describes the *type* of money (owed), but the number describes the *amount*.
mappingData.criticalThinking: A student demonstrates deep understanding when they can explain that while $-40$ is *less* than $-10$, the *absolute value* $|-40|$ is *greater* than $|-10|$. Self-correction occurs when a student realizes that absolute value "strips away" the direction to reveal only the raw distance.

7-g-a-2-the-spaghetti-bridge.title: The Spaghetti Bridge
7-g-a-2-the-spaghetti-bridge.standardDescription: Draw (freehand, with ruler and protractor, and with technology) geometric shapes with given conditions. Focus on constructing triangles from three measures of angles or sides, noticing when the conditions determine a unique triangle, more than one triangle, or no triangle.
7-g-a-2-the-spaghetti-bridge.description: I'm trying to build this triangle support for a bridge using three pieces of dry spaghetti, but no matter how I move the
7-g-a-2-the-spaghetti-bridge.problemStatement: "I'm trying to build this triangle support for a bridge using three pieces of dry spaghetti, but no matter how I move them around, the ends won't touch to close the shape. One piece is 10 inches long, and the other two are only 4 inches and 5 inches—do you think it's even possible to make them form a triangle without breaking them?"
7-g-a-2-the-spaghetti-bridge.teachingPrompt: Help Zippy understand The Spaghetti Bridge by teaching the key mathematical ideas step by step.
misconceptions:
Length Sum Ignorance: (Type: structural_misunderstanding)   
Students may believe that any three lengths can form a triangle as long as they are tilted at the correct angles.

Endpoint Gap Blindness: (Type: partial_concept_model)   
A student might think the shape is a triangle even if the vertices don't perfectly meet, viewing "triangularity" as a general look rather than a closed constraint.

Largest Side Bias: (Type: additive_vs_multiplicative_confusion)   
Students might assume that as long as the longest side is less than the total sum of all sides, it will work, failing to compare it specifically to the sum of the two shorter sides.
7-g-a-2-the-spaghetti-bridge.aiIntro: Hi! I'm Zippy! I need help with "The Spaghetti Bridge". "I'm trying to build this triangle support for a bridge using three pieces of dry spaghetti, but no matter how I move them around, the ends won't touch to close the shape. One piece is 10 inches long, and the other two are only 4 inches and 5 inches—do you think it's even possible to make them form a triangle without breaking them?". I was thinking about it and got confused because length sum ignorance. Can you help me understand?

One piece is 10 inches long, and the other two are only 4 inches and 5 inches—do you think it's even possible to make them form a triangle without breaking them?"
misconceptions:
Length Sum Ignorance: (Type: structural_misunderstanding)   
Students may believe that any three lengths can form a triangle as long as they are tilted at the correct angles.

Endpoint Gap Blindness: (Type: partial_concept_model)   
A student might think the shape is a triangle even if the vertices don't perfectly meet, viewing "triangularity" as a general look rather than a closed constraint.

Largest Side Bias: (Type: additive_vs_multiplicative_confusion)   
Students might assume that as long as the longest side is less than the total sum of all sides, it will work, failing to compare it specifically to the sum of the two shorter sides.
sections.patternRecognition:
- "Wait, if we keep the 10-inch piece but swap the others for two 6-inch pieces, does it suddenly stop being impossible?"
- "Does it matter which piece we put on the bottom, or does that 'gap' stay exactly the same size?"
- "Is there a specific total length the two short pieces have to hit before they finally start to reach each other?"
sections.generalization:
Question (Always / Sometimes / Never)
"I was thinking... if you have two short sticks that add up to exactly the same length as the long stick, they'll always be able to bridge the gap and make a triangle, right?"
sections.inference:
"So I tried it again with new pieces: one is 12cm and another is 7cm. When I used a 3cm piece for the last side, it failed, but when I used an 8cm piece, it worked easily. Is there a way to know the shortest and longest possible lengths that third piece could be so I don't keep picking duds?"

**Prediction target:** The range of possible whole-number lengths for the third piece to successfully close the triangle.

claims: Students can determine the necessary relationship between side lengths (Triangle Inequality Theorem) required to form a closed geometric figure.
Students can predict whether a unique triangle, multiple triangles, or no triangle can be formed based on given side constraints.
mappingData.evidence: Evidence is found when students move from trying to "angle" the sides to realize that the sum of the two shorter segments must strictly exceed the longest segment. This is demonstrated when they identify that a sum equal to the third side results in a flat line (degenerate triangle) rather than a shape with area.
mappingData.criticalThinking: Deep understanding is revealed when a student stops "trial and error" with specific numbers and instead creates a testable rule, such as checking if the difference and sum of the two known sides bound the third.

7-ns-a-1-a-the-zero-sum-scavenger-hunt.title: The Zero-Sum Scavenger Hunt
7-ns-a-1-a-the-zero-sum-scavenger-hunt.standardDescription: Describe situations in which opposite quantities combine to make 0. For example, a hydrogen atom has 0 charge because its two constituents are oppositely charged.
7-ns-a-1-a-the-zero-sum-scavenger-hunt.description: We’re playing this school-wide scavenger hunt where you get points for finding hidden items but lose points for taking t
7-ns-a-1-a-the-zero-sum-scavenger-hunt.problemStatement: "We’re playing this school-wide scavenger hunt where you get points for finding hidden items but lose points for taking too long, and my team’s digital tracker just flashed a big 'ZERO' for our current score. Does that mean we haven't found anything at all, or is there a way we could have found a bunch of stuff and still ended up with absolutely nothing on the screen?"
7-ns-a-1-a-the-zero-sum-scavenger-hunt.teachingPrompt: Help Zippy understand The Zero-Sum Scavenger Hunt by teaching the key mathematical ideas step by step.
misconceptions: Null Set Confusion: [The student might assume that a total of zero can only exist if no actions have taken place (the 'starting at zero' state).]
Magnitude Overestimation: [A student may believe that if you perform a 'big' action, like finding a 50-point item, the score can never return to zero because 50 is such a large number.]
Directional Misunderstanding: [A student might think that 'losing points' just stops your score from growing rather than actively moving the value back toward or past zero.]
7-ns-a-1-a-the-zero-sum-scavenger-hunt.aiIntro: Hi! I'm Zippy! I need help with "The Zero-Sum Scavenger Hunt". "We’re playing this school-wide scavenger hunt where you get points for finding hidden items but lose points for taking too long, and my team’s digital tracker just flashed a big 'ZERO' for our current score. Does that mean we haven't found anything at all, or is there a way we could have found a bunch of stuff and still ended up with absolutely nothing on the screen?". I was thinking about it and got confused because null set confusion. Can you help me understand?

Does that mean we haven't found anything at all, or is there a way we could have found a bunch of stuff and still ended up with absolutely nothing on the screen?"
misconceptions: Null Set Confusion: [The student might assume that a total of zero can only exist if no actions have taken place (the 'starting at zero' state).]
Magnitude Overestimation: [A student may believe that if you perform a 'big' action, like finding a 50-point item, the score can never return to zero because 50 is such a large number.]
Directional Misunderstanding: [A student might think that 'losing points' just stops your score from growing rather than actively moving the value back toward or past zero.]
sections.patternRecognition:
- "If you found a hidden gold star worth 15 points, what would have to happen with the 'time penalty' to make that 15 disappear instantly?"
- "Is there a specific 'match' for every positive point that would drag the score back to the exact middle?"
- "Does it matter if you lose the points first and then find the item, or do they always cancel each other out the same way?"
sections.generalization:
Question (Always / Sometimes / Never)
"If I have a massive pile of 'positive' points, is it ALWAYS possible to find a 'negative' amount that lands me exactly back at zero, or are there some numbers that just don't have a perfect opposite?"
sections.inference:
"So I was reading about these deep-sea divers, and they said their 'net elevation' was zero because they started at the surface, dove way down to a ship wreck, and then came all the way back up to the boat. If they dove down exactly 42.5 meters to find a treasure chest, what would their very next move have to be to make their total change in position zero again?"

**Prediction target:** The specific direction and distance of the divers' return trip.

claims: Students can identify and describe real-world scenarios where opposite forces or quantities result in a net value of zero.
Students can explain the relationship between a rational number and its additive inverse in a situational context.
mappingData.evidence: Evidence is elicited when students describe the "canceling" effect of two distinct actions (e.g., +15 and -15). The task forces reasoning by presenting a result (zero) and requiring the student to reconstruct the balanced actions that could have led to it, rather than just asking them to solve a sum.
mappingData.criticalThinking: A student demonstrates deep understanding when they move from saying "they did nothing" to saying "they did two things that balanced out." This shift is observable when a student uses the term "opposite" or "inverse" to describe how a specific loss perfectly offsets a specific gain.

6-rp-a-3-d-the-giant-blueprints.title: The Giant Blueprints
6-rp-a-3-d-the-giant-blueprints.standardDescription: Use ratio reasoning to convert measurement units; manipulate and transform units appropriately when multiplying or dividing quantities.
6-rp-a-3-d-the-giant-blueprints.description: I'm helping my dad build a giant model of a superhero tower, and the instructions say the base needs to be exactly 108 i
6-rp-a-3-d-the-giant-blueprints.problemStatement: "I'm helping my dad build a giant model of a superhero tower, and the instructions say the base needs to be exactly 108 inches wide. But my tape measure only shows feet, and I don't want to sit there counting every single little tick mark until I hit 108. If there are 12 inches in every single foot, is there a way to figure out exactly where the 'foot' mark is on my tape measure without just counting by ones?"
6-rp-a-3-d-the-giant-blueprints.teachingPrompt: Help Zippy understand The Giant Blueprints by teaching the key mathematical ideas step by step.
misconceptions: Additive Conversion: Students may think they need to subtract 12 from 108 repeatedly or add 12 to 1 and assume the relationship stays the same by adding.
Inverse Operation: Students might multiply 108 by 12 because they know the numbers are related, resulting in an answer in 'square inches' or a massive number of feet.
Unit Magnitude Bias: Students may assume that because 'inches' are smaller than 'feet,' the final number for feet should be larger than 108.
6-rp-a-3-d-the-giant-blueprints.aiIntro: Hi! I'm Zippy! I need help with "The Giant Blueprints". "I'm helping my dad build a giant model of a superhero tower, and the instructions say the base needs to be exactly 108 inches wide. But my tape measure only shows feet, and I don't want to sit there counting every single little tick mark until I hit 108. I was thinking about it and got confused because additive conversion. Can you help me understand?

sections.studentPrompt: "I'm helping my dad build a giant model of a superhero tower, and the instructions say the base needs to be exactly 108 inches wide. But my tape measure only shows feet, and I don't want to sit there counting every single little tick mark until I hit 108. If there are 12 inches in every single foot, is there a way to figure out exactly where the 'foot' mark is on my tape measure without just counting by ones?"
misconceptions: Additive Conversion: Students may think they need to subtract 12 from 108 repeatedly or add 12 to 1 and assume the relationship stays the same by adding.
Inverse Operation: Students might multiply 108 by 12 because they know the numbers are related, resulting in an answer in 'square inches' or a massive number of feet.
Unit Magnitude Bias: Students may assume that because 'inches' are smaller than 'feet,' the final number for feet should be larger than 108.
sections.patternRecognition:
- "If you have 24 inches, how many of those 12-inch 'foot' chunks are you actually holding?"
- "Wait—does the number of inches inside a single foot ever change as the tower gets taller?"
- "Okay, so if the number of inches is getting way bigger, what is happening to the number of feet to keep the length the same?"
sections.generalization:
Question (Always / Sometimes / Never)
"I have a half-confident hunch that if you're switching from a small unit to a bigger one, your total number is ALWAYS going to get smaller... or is there some weird measurement out there where the number actually gets bigger when the unit does?"
sections.inference:
"I found this other part of the tower that needs to be 15 feet long, but now I only have a small ruler that's marked in inches. If 1 foot is the same as 12 inches and 2 feet is the same as 24, do you think there's a trick to find the total inches for 15 feet without drawing 15 separate rulers? And like, would that same trick help us find the inches for ANY number of feet?"

**Prediction target:** The total number of inches in 15 feet (180 inches) using the constant ratio of 12:1.

claims: Students can use ratio reasoning to convert between measurement units by identifying the unit rate (e.g., 12 in / 1 ft).
Students can transform units appropriately by choosing multiplication or division based on the direction of the conversion.
mappingData.evidence: Evidence is observed when students describe the 108 inches as "nine groups of 12." By forcing a comparison between a small unit (inches) and a large unit (feet), the task design forces students to use the ratio 12:1 as a conversion factor rather than just a memorized math fact.
mappingData.criticalThinking: A student demonstrates deep understanding when they explain that the physical length stays the same, so as the "ruler size" (unit) gets 12 times bigger, the "number of pieces" (count) must get 12 times smaller. This shift from blind calculation to conservation of length distinguishes deep proportional reasoning.

7-rp-a-the-coffee-shop-chemist.title: The Coffee Shop Chemist
7-rp-a-the-coffee-shop-chemist.standardDescription: Analyze proportional relationships and use them to solve real-world and mathematical problems, including calculating unit rates with fractions, identifying proportional relationships in tables and graphs, and representing them with equations.
7-rp-a-the-coffee-shop-chemist.description: I'm working at a local coffee shop, and we have a 'Secret Sauce' for our lattes
7-rp-a-the-coffee-shop-chemist.problemStatement:
"I'm working at a local coffee shop, and we have a 'Secret Sauce' for our lattes. The recipe says to mix $\frac{1}{2}$ cup of cocoa for every $\frac{3}{4}$ cup of hot water. My manager wants to know the **unit rate** (how much cocoa per 1 full cup of water). 

Also, we have a table showing the cost of different sized lattes: 8oz for \$3.20, 12oz for \$4.80, and 16oz for \$6.40. Is the price **proportional** to the size? If I draw a graph of this, will it go through the $(0,0)$ point, and how do I write an equation ($y = kx$) so I can tell a customer the price of a 20oz latte instantly?"
7-rp-a-the-coffee-shop-chemist.teachingPrompt: Help Zippy understand The Coffee Shop Chemist by teaching the key mathematical ideas step by step.
misconceptions: The Fraction Flip: When calculating unit rates with fractions (e.g., $\frac{1}{2} \div \frac{3}{4}$), students often forget to multiply by the reciprocal or they flip the wrong fraction. They might calculate $\frac{3}{4}$ cups of cocoa per water instead of $\frac{2}{3}$.

The "Origin" Oversight: Students often identify a "straight line" on a graph and assume it's proportional, forgetting that a proportional relationship **must** pass through the origin $(0,0)$. For example, a phone plan that costs \$20 plus \$0.10 per minute is a straight line, but it is *not* proportional.

Constant of Proportionality ($k$) Confusion: A student might calculate $x/y$ instead of $y/x$ to find $k$. In the latte example, they might say the constant is 2.5 (ounces per dollar) instead of 0.4 (dollars per ounce), making their equation $y = 2.5x$ predict a 20oz latte costs \$50!
7-rp-a-the-coffee-shop-chemist.aiIntro: Hi! I'm Zippy! I need help with "The Coffee Shop Chemist". "I'm working at a local coffee shop, and we have a 'Secret Sauce' for our lattes. The recipe says to mix $\frac{1}{2}$ cup of cocoa for every $\frac{3}{4}$ cup of hot water. I was thinking about it and got confused because the fraction flip. Can you help me understand?

sections.studentPrompt:
"I'm working at a local coffee shop, and we have a 'Secret Sauce' for our lattes. The recipe says to mix $\frac{1}{2}$ cup of cocoa for every $\frac{3}{4}$ cup of hot water. My manager wants to know the **unit rate** (how much cocoa per 1 full cup of water). 

Also, we have a table showing the cost of different sized lattes: 8oz for \$3.20, 12oz for \$4.80, and 16oz for \$6.40. Is the price **proportional** to the size? If I draw a graph of this, will it go through the $(0,0)$ point, and how do I write an equation ($y = kx$) so I can tell a customer the price of a 20oz latte instantly?"
misconceptions: The Fraction Flip: When calculating unit rates with fractions (e.g., $\frac{1}{2} \div \frac{3}{4}$), students often forget to multiply by the reciprocal or they flip the wrong fraction. They might calculate $\frac{3}{4}$ cups of cocoa per water instead of $\frac{2}{3}$.

The "Origin" Oversight: Students often identify a "straight line" on a graph and assume it's proportional, forgetting that a proportional relationship **must** pass through the origin $(0,0)$. For example, a phone plan that costs \$20 plus \$0.10 per minute is a straight line, but it is *not* proportional.

Constant of Proportionality ($k$) Confusion: A student might calculate $x/y$ instead of $y/x$ to find $k$. In the latte example, they might say the constant is 2.5 (ounces per dollar) instead of 0.4 (dollars per ounce), making their equation $y = 2.5x$ predict a 20oz latte costs \$50!
sections.patternRecognition:
- "If you buy **zero** ounces of coffee, should you pay **zero** dollars? If the answer is yes, what does that tell you about your graph's starting point?"
- "Wait—if you divide the Price by the Ounces for every row in your table ($3.20 \div 8$, $4.80 \div 12$), do you get the exact same 'magic number' every time?"
- "In the recipe, if you wanted 1 cup of water, would you need more or less than $\frac{1}{2}$ cup of cocoa? Does your unit rate reflect that?"
sections.generalization:
Question (Always / Sometimes / Never)
"I have a hunch that if a relationship is proportional, the graph will ALWAYS be a straight line... but is every straight line ALWAYS a proportional relationship?"
sections.inference:
"We found the equation for latte prices is $y = 0.4x$, where $y$ is the cost and $x$ is the ounces. 
* What does the point $(1, 0.4)$ represent in the 'real world' of the coffee shop? 
* If the shop adds a \$0.50 'service fee' to every order, is the new relationship ($y = 0.4x + 0.5$) still proportional? How would the graph change?"

**Prediction target:** Identifying $(1, 0.4)$ as the unit rate (\$0.40 per ounce). Recognizing that adding a constant fee makes the relationship non-proportional because it no longer passes through $(0,0)$.

claims: Students can compute unit rates associated with ratios of fractions.
Students can identify proportional relationships in tables, graphs, and verbal descriptions.
Students can represent proportional relationships by equations of the form $y = kx$.
mappingData.evidence: Evidence is gathered when a student successfully calculates the unit rate for cocoa ($\frac{2}{3}$ cup per cup of water) and identifies $k = 0.4$ for the latte pricing. The task forces this by using complex fractions and decimal-based pricing, preventing simple whole-number "guessing."
mappingData.criticalThinking: A student demonstrates deep understanding when they can explain that $k$ is the "unit rate" and is visible on a graph at the point $(1, k)$. Self-correction occurs when a student realizes that if the ratios $y/x$ are not constant across the table, the relationship cannot be represented by a single $y = kx$ equation.

7-sp-c-8-the-double-drip-cafe.title: The Double-Drip Cafe
7-sp-c-8-the-double-drip-cafe.standardDescription: Find probabilities of compound events using organized lists, tables, tree diagrams, and simulation.
7-sp-c-8-the-double-drip-cafe.description: The cafe down the street gives out a mystery sticker with every drink, but they only have two designs—a sun and a moon—a
7-sp-c-8-the-double-drip-cafe.problemStatement: The cafe down the street gives out a mystery sticker with every drink, but they only have two designs—a sun and a moon—and they just grab them out of a box without looking. If I buy two drinks today, is it a 50/50 shot that I'll end up with one of each sticker, or is there some other way this could go?
7-sp-c-8-the-double-drip-cafe.teachingPrompt: Help Zippy understand The Double-Drip Cafe by teaching the key mathematical ideas step by step.
misconceptions: The "Order Doesn't Matter" Trap: The student thinks there are only three outcomes (two suns, two moons, or one of each) and assumes they are all equally likely.
Single-Event Bias: The student focuses only on the 1/2 probability of a single sticker and fails to see how the two independent events multiply the total possibilities.
Sample Space Omission: The student forgets to account for different sequences, like getting a sun then a moon versus getting a moon then a sun.
7-sp-c-8-the-double-drip-cafe.aiIntro: Hi! I'm Zippy! I need help with "The Double-Drip Cafe". The cafe down the street gives out a mystery sticker with every drink, but they only have two designs—a sun and a moon—and they just grab them out of a box without looking. If I buy two drinks today, is it a 50/50 shot that I'll end up with one of each sticker, or is there some other way this could go?. I was thinking about it and got confused because the "order doesn't matter" trap. Can you help me understand?

sections.studentPrompt: The cafe down the street gives out a mystery sticker with every drink, but they only have two designs—a sun and a moon—and they just grab them out of a box without looking. If I buy two drinks today, is it a 50/50 shot that I'll end up with one of each sticker, or is there some other way this could go?
misconceptions: The "Order Doesn't Matter" Trap: The student thinks there are only three outcomes (two suns, two moons, or one of each) and assumes they are all equally likely.
Single-Event Bias: The student focuses only on the 1/2 probability of a single sticker and fails to see how the two independent events multiply the total possibilities.
Sample Space Omission: The student forgets to account for different sequences, like getting a sun then a moon versus getting a moon then a sun.
sections.patternRecognition:
- If you kept track of every single pair of stickers people walked out with, which specific combinations would you see changing from person to person? 
- Does the fact that there are always exactly two stickers in a "set" change how many total ways the morning could go? 
- How do the "Sun-Moon" pairs and the "Moon-Sun" pairs connect when we're trying to figure out the total number of ways to get stickers?
sections.generalization:
Question (Always / Sometimes / Never)
"Wait, so if there are two options for the first thing and two options for the second thing, does that mean the total number of combinations is ALWAYS just going to be those two numbers added together... or is there some other rule that happens when we combine them?"
sections.inference:
I went to a different shop that has three different stickers—a star, a planet, and a comet—and they still give you two per order. I saw one person get a star and a planet, and another person got two comets! Do you think we could find every single possible pair without missing any, and would "one of each" still be the most likely thing to happen? 

**Prediction target:** The total number of unique sticker pairings possible when the number of designs increases to three.

claims: The student can identify and represent all possible outcomes in a compound event sample space.
The student can determine the probability of a specific compound event by comparing favorable outcomes to the total sample space.
mappingData.evidence: The task design requires students to move beyond a 50/50 guess by visualizing or listing the actual sequences (SS, SM, MS, MM).  Evidence of deep reasoning is found when a student recognizes that "one of each" occurs in two different ways out of four total possibilities, confirming the 50% probability through structure rather than intuition.
mappingData.criticalThinking: One observable behavior of deep understanding is when a student self-corrects their initial list to include "swapped" pairs (like Moon-Sun) after realizing the order of the two drinks creates distinct outcomes in the sample space.

6-sp-a-2-the-shape-of-the-class.title: The Shape of the Class
6-sp-a-2-the-shape-of-the-class.standardDescription: Understand that a set of data collected to answer a statistical question has a distribution which can be described by its center, spread, and overall shape.
6-sp-a-2-the-shape-of-the-class.description: I collected data on how many books my classmates read over summer break
6-sp-a-2-the-shape-of-the-class.problemStatement: "I collected data on how many books my classmates read over summer break. I made a dot plot, and most of the dots are clustered around 2 and 3 books, but there's one lonely dot way out at 15! My teacher told me to describe the 'distribution.' I know the 'center' is somewhere near 3, but what does she mean by 'shape' and 'spread'? Is my data a 'circle' or a 'square'? And how does that one kid who read 15 books change the whole picture?"
6-sp-a-2-the-shape-of-the-class.teachingPrompt: Help Zippy understand The Shape of the Class by teaching the key mathematical ideas step by step.
misconceptions: Shape as Geometric Figures: Students often hear the word "shape" and think of literal geometry (circles, triangles). They struggle to use statistical terms like **symmetrical**, **skewed**, **peaked**, or **gaps**.

Center vs. Most Frequent: A student might think the "center" is always the middle of the number line (e.g., if the scale is 0–20, the center must be 10), rather than where the actual data points are concentrated.
Ignoring the 'Tail': When a distribution has an outlier (like the 15 books), students often ignore it when describing the "spread," thinking the spread only counts where "most" of the people are.
6-sp-a-2-the-shape-of-the-class.aiIntro: Hi! I'm Zippy! I need help with "The Shape of the Class". "I collected data on how many books my classmates read over summer break. I made a dot plot, and most of the dots are clustered around 2 and 3 books, but there's one lonely dot way out at 15! My teacher told me to describe the 'distribution. I was thinking about it and got confused because shape as geometric figures. Can you help me understand?

sections.studentPrompt: "I collected data on how many books my classmates read over summer break. I made a dot plot, and most of the dots are clustered around 2 and 3 books, but there's one lonely dot way out at 15! My teacher told me to describe the 'distribution.' I know the 'center' is somewhere near 3, but what does she mean by 'shape' and 'spread'? Is my data a 'circle' or a 'square'? And how does that one kid who read 15 books change the whole picture?"
misconceptions: Shape as Geometric Figures: Students often hear the word "shape" and think of literal geometry (circles, triangles). They struggle to use statistical terms like **symmetrical**, **skewed**, **peaked**, or **gaps**.

Center vs. Most Frequent: A student might think the "center" is always the middle of the number line (e.g., if the scale is 0–20, the center must be 10), rather than where the actual data points are concentrated.
Ignoring the 'Tail': When a distribution has an outlier (like the 15 books), students often ignore it when describing the "spread," thinking the spread only counts where "most" of the people are.
sections.patternRecognition:
- "If you look at the dots from a distance, does the 'hill' look even on both sides, or does it have a long 'tail' stretching out to the right?"
- "Wait—if everyone in class read exactly 3 books, would there be any 'spread' at all? What does it mean if the dots are scattered from 0 all the way to 15?"
- "Does the 'center' feel like the balance point of a seesaw? If you put a heavy weight at 15, which way does the center have to slide?"
sections.generalization:
Question (Always / Sometimes / Never)
"I have a hunch that the 'center' of a data set is ALWAYS the same number as the 'most popular' answer... or can the center be a number that nobody actually picked?"
sections.inference:
"Imagine we surveyed a class of professional speed-readers. Their dot plot is perfectly **symmetrical** with a center at 50 books and a very **small spread** (everyone read between 48 and 52). How would that graph look different from our class graph that is **skewed** and has a **large spread**? Which group is more 'predictable'?"

**Prediction target:** Identifying that a symmetrical, low-spread graph looks like a tall, thin spike, while a skewed, high-spread graph is flatter and stretched out. Recognizing that lower variability (spread) makes data more predictable.

claims: Students can identify the center of a data distribution (mean or median).
Students can describe the spread (variability) of a data set.
Students can describe the overall shape of a data distribution (e.g., skewed, symmetrical, bell-shaped).
mappingData.evidence: Evidence is gathered when a student looks at a dot plot and identifies a "peak" at 3, a "gap" between 5 and 15, and a "right skew" caused by the outlier. The task forces this by using a non-symmetrical data set, which requires more nuanced description than just finding an average.
mappingData.criticalThinking: A student demonstrates deep understanding when they can explain that the "15" pulls the mean to the right but might not affect the median as much. Self-correction occurs when a student realizes that "spread" isn't just one number, but a description of how much the data varies from the center.

6-sp-b-5-a-the-headcount-challenge.title: The Headcount Challenge
6-sp-b-5-a-the-headcount-challenge.standardDescription: Summarize numerical data sets in relation to their context, such as by: Reporting the number of observations.
6-sp-b-5-a-the-headcount-challenge.description: I'm collecting data for the school cafeteria to see how many chocolate milks are sold each day
6-sp-b-5-a-the-headcount-challenge.problemStatement: "I'm collecting data for the school cafeteria to see how many chocolate milks are sold each day. I checked the logs for Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, and Thursday. My friend says my 'number of observations' is 4. But then I looked at the numbers: we sold 45, 50, 48, and 52 milks. Now I'm confused—is my number of observations 4, or is it the total of all those milks (195)? Why does it matter how many days I checked if I already have the big total?"
6-sp-b-5-a-the-headcount-challenge.teachingPrompt: Help Zippy understand The Headcount Challenge by teaching the key mathematical ideas step by step.
misconceptions: Sum vs. Count: Students often confuse the **sum of the data** (adding all values together) with the **number of observations** (how many pieces of data were collected). They might report $n=195$ instead of $n=4$.

The "Value" Distraction: A student might see a large number in the data set (like 52 milks) and assume that the number of observations must be related to the size of the numbers, rather than the count of the data points.

Zero as "Nothing": If no milks were sold on Friday, the data point is 0. Students often skip this, thinking it doesn't "count" as an observation. In statistics, a zero is a very important observation that must be included in the total count ($n$).
6-sp-b-5-a-the-headcount-challenge.aiIntro: Hi! I'm Zippy! I need help with "The Headcount Challenge". "I'm collecting data for the school cafeteria to see how many chocolate milks are sold each day. I checked the logs for Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, and Thursday. I was thinking about it and got confused because sum vs. Can you help me understand?

sections.studentPrompt: "I'm collecting data for the school cafeteria to see how many chocolate milks are sold each day. I checked the logs for Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, and Thursday. My friend says my 'number of observations' is 4. But then I looked at the numbers: we sold 45, 50, 48, and 52 milks. Now I'm confused—is my number of observations 4, or is it the total of all those milks (195)? Why does it matter how many days I checked if I already have the big total?"
misconceptions: Sum vs. Count: Students often confuse the **sum of the data** (adding all values together) with the **number of observations** (how many pieces of data were collected). They might report $n=195$ instead of $n=4$.

The "Value" Distraction: A student might see a large number in the data set (like 52 milks) and assume that the number of observations must be related to the size of the numbers, rather than the count of the data points.

Zero as "Nothing": If no milks were sold on Friday, the data point is 0. Students often skip this, thinking it doesn't "count" as an observation. In statistics, a zero is a very important observation that must be included in the total count ($n$).
sections.patternRecognition:
- "If you want to know the 'average' milk per day, do you divide the total by the number of milks, or the number of days you watched the cafeteria?"
- "Wait—if you only watched for one day, would you trust your data as much as if you watched for 100 days? Does having more 'observations' make your story more reliable?"
- "If I give you a list of 10 test scores, how many times did someone actually sit down and take a test?"
sections.generalization:
Question (Always / Sometimes / Never)
"I have a hunch that the more observations ($n$) you have, the ALWAYS more 'accurate' your average will be for the whole school... or can you have too many observations?"
sections.inference:
"Imagine the cafeteria manager says, 'I need a bigger sample size!' If you keep track of the milk for the next 3 weeks, will your 'number of observations' go up or down? And how does that change the way you'd calculate the **Mean** later on?"

**Prediction target:** Identifying that $n$ will increase (from 4 to 19 observations, assuming school days). Recognizing that a larger $n$ provides a more stable denominator for calculating the mean.

claims: Students can identify and report the total number of observations ($n$) in a given data set.
Students understand that each data point represents one specific instance or "observation" within the context.
mappingData.evidence: Evidence is gathered when a student correctly identifies that $n=4$ for the four days of milk logs. The task forces this by providing "large" data values ($45, 50$, etc.) to contrast against the "small" number of observations, helping the student separate the *size* of the data from the *count* of the data.
mappingData.criticalThinking: A student demonstrates deep understanding when they can explain that the "number of observations" represents the "sample size." Self-correction occurs when a student realizes that if they forget to count a day where 0 milks were sold, their "average" will be incorrectly high because they divided by the wrong $n$.

7-ee-a-the-discount-dilemma.title: The Discount Dilemma
7-ee-a-the-discount-dilemma.standardDescription: Apply properties of operations as strategies to add, subtract, factor, and expand linear expressions with rational coefficients. Understand that rewriting an expression in different forms in a problem context can shed light on the problem and how the quantities in it are related.
7-ee-a-the-discount-dilemma.description: My friend and I are looking at these skateboards that are normally [math] dollars, but the shop is having a "20% off eve
7-ee-a-the-discount-dilemma.problemStatement: My friend and I are looking at these skateboards that are normally $x$ dollars, but the shop is having a "20% off everything" sale. I think the price is just $x - 0.20x$, but my friend says it’s way easier to just calculate $0.80x$. If we both start with the same original price, are we actually going to end up with the same total at the register?
7-ee-a-the-discount-dilemma.teachingPrompt: Help Zippy understand The Discount Dilemma by teaching the key mathematical ideas step by step.
misconceptions: Additive vs. Multiplicative Confusion: The student tries to subtract the 20% as a flat 20 cents ($x - 0.20$) rather than 20% of the variable $x$.
Term Detachment: The student treats the $0.20x$ as a separate entity and fails to realize that $x$ has an implicit coefficient of 1.
Operational Misinterpretation: The student believes that $0.80x$ represents an 80% discount rather than the remaining 80% of the original cost.
7-ee-a-the-discount-dilemma.aiIntro: Hi! I'm Zippy! I need help with "The Discount Dilemma". My friend and I are looking at these skateboards that are normally $x$ dollars, but the shop is having a "20% off everything" sale. I think the price is just $x - 0. I was thinking about it and got confused because additive vs. Can you help me understand?

sections.studentPrompt: My friend and I are looking at these skateboards that are normally $x$ dollars, but the shop is having a "20% off everything" sale. I think the price is just $x - 0.20x$, but my friend says it’s way easier to just calculate $0.80x$. If we both start with the same original price, are we actually going to end up with the same total at the register?
misconceptions: Additive vs. Multiplicative Confusion: The student tries to subtract the 20% as a flat 20 cents ($x - 0.20$) rather than 20% of the variable $x$.
Term Detachment: The student treats the $0.20x$ as a separate entity and fails to realize that $x$ has an implicit coefficient of 1.
Operational Misinterpretation: The student believes that $0.80x$ represents an 80% discount rather than the remaining 80% of the original cost.
sections.patternRecognition:
- If the skateboard was exactly $100, what happens to the numbers in my way of doing it versus my friend's way?
- Does that "0.20" part always have to be attached to the $x$, or can it just hang out by itself?
- Why does my friend's version only have one term while mine has two?
sections.generalization:
Question (Always / Sometimes / Never)
"Wait, so if you're taking a percentage off, is it ALWAYS the same as just multiplying by whatever percentage is left over, or does that only work for certain numbers?"
sections.inference:
So I just saw a sign for a different shop that says "Buy 3 skateboards, get 10% off the total." I was thinking the total would be $(x + x + x) - 0.10(x + x + x)$, but then I saw a skater write down $0.90(3x)$ on his hand. Do you think those two ways are actually talking about the same amount of money? And if I wanted to buy 5 boards instead, is there a way to know the total without writing out the whole long subtraction thing?

**Prediction target:** The simplified decimal coefficient for the total cost of 5 skateboards with a 10% discount.

claims: The student can apply properties of operations to add, subtract, and expand linear expressions with rational coefficients.
The student can interpret how different forms of an expression represent the same relationship between quantities in a context.
mappingData.evidence: Evidence is provided when a student demonstrates that $1x - 0.20x$ simplifies to $(1 - 0.20)x$ using the distributive property. Deep reasoning is shown when the student explains that $0.80x$ represents the "remaining portion" while $x - 0.20x$ represents the "original minus the discount," proving they see the mathematical structure behind the context.
mappingData.criticalThinking: A student demonstrates high-level thinking by self-correcting the "invisible 1" coefficient in front of a variable when combining like terms. This shift from seeing $x$ as a symbol to seeing $x$ as $1x$ allows them to bridge the gap between two-term subtraction and single-term multiplication.

7-g-b-6-the-glass-aquarium-gap.title: The Glass Aquarium Gap
7-g-b-6-the-glass-aquarium-gap.standardDescription: Solve real-world and mathematical problems involving area, volume and surface area of two- and three-dimensional objects composed of triangles, quadrilaterals, polygons, cubes, and right prisms.
7-g-b-6-the-glass-aquarium-gap.description: So I’m trying to figure out how much glass I actually need to buy to build this open-top fish tank that is 2 feet long,
7-g-b-6-the-glass-aquarium-gap.problemStatement: So I’m trying to figure out how much glass I actually need to buy to build this open-top fish tank that is 2 feet long, 1 foot wide, and 1 foot tall, but my older brother says the "amount of space inside" and the "amount of glass" aren't even the same number. If I double the height to make it a 2-foot cube instead, does that mean I suddenly need exactly double the glass I started with?
7-g-b-6-the-glass-aquarium-gap.teachingPrompt: Help Zippy understand The Glass Aquarium Gap by teaching the key mathematical ideas step by step.
misconceptions: Volume vs. Surface Area Confusion: The student assumes that the numerical value for the space inside (volume) should be identical to the material needed for the walls (surface area).
Neglecting the Open Top: The student uses the standard formula for a closed prism ($SA = 2lw + 2lh + 2wh$) and includes a sixth face that doesn't exist in a "tank" scenario.
Linear Scaling Fallacy: The student believes that doubling one dimension (height) results in a proportional doubling of the total surface area, failing to account for the base staying constant.
7-g-b-6-the-glass-aquarium-gap.aiIntro: Hi! I'm Zippy! I need help with "The Glass Aquarium Gap". If I double the height to make it a 2-foot cube instead, does that mean I suddenly need exactly double the glass I started with?. I was thinking about it and got confused because volume vs. Can you help me understand?
